# Lives of Faith — Full Biographical Content

> Complete plain-text biographies for every person on livesoffaith.org, for AI assistants and search tools that cannot execute the JavaScript that renders these pages in a browser. Canonical, interactive versions of each page (with portraits, timelines, memorials, and copy-to-clipboard tools) are linked below. All content is Christ-centred and grace-focused — see the summary in llms.txt for full editorial scope and exclusions.

Generated 2026-07-04. 136 people.

---

## A. W. Tozer (1897–1963)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=aw-tozer
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/A._W._Tozer
Nationality: American · Tradition: Christian and Missionary Alliance · Region: North America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: prayer, worship, grace, devotion

Aiden Wilson Tozer was born in rural Pennsylvania in 1897 and grew up in Akron, Ohio, with no particular interest in religion. At seventeen, walking home from work, he passed a street preacher who called out that anyone could be saved simply by praying, ‘God be merciful to me, a sinner.’ Tozer went home, climbed to the attic alone, and did exactly that. He never forgot that the whole of his salvation began with nothing more than a stranger's plain words and his own honest prayer.

He never attended seminary, teaching himself theology instead through relentless reading in libraries and secondhand bookshops. Entering ministry with the Christian and Missionary Alliance in 1919, he became pastor of Southside Alliance Church in Chicago in 1928, where his Sunday evening services drew students from across the city's colleges for thirty-one years. From 1950 he also edited the denomination's magazine. His books, especially The Pursuit of God (1948) and The Knowledge of the Holy (1961), pressed one relentless question on comfortable Christians: did they actually know the God they claimed to worship, or merely know about him?

Tozer's answer always returned to that Akron attic — that nearness to God is not achieved by religious performance but received, like his own conversion, as a gift simply asked for and simply given.

---

## Abraham Kuyper (1837–1920)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=abraham-kuyper
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abraham_Kuyper
Nationality: Dutch · Tradition: Reformed / Neo-Calvinist · Region: Europe · Era: 19th Century
Topics: grace, faithfulness, Scripture, sovereignty of God, service

Abraham Kuyper was born in 1837 in Maassluis, Holland, the son of a Reformed pastor. Brilliant and ambitious, he earned his doctorate at Leiden in 1862 already leaning toward the fashionable liberal theology of his day, which treated Scripture as human literature rather than God’s word. His first pastorate, in the small village of Beesd, would change that.

There he met Pietje Baltus, a servant girl in his congregation who refused to shake his hand, telling him plainly that he did not yet believe what the Bible actually taught. Her blunt conviction, and his own return to serious study of Scripture, turned Kuyper from fashionable doubt to confident faith in the gospel of grace. He went on to found the newspaper De Standaard, lead a body of churches out of a compromised state denomination in 1886, and establish the Vrije Universiteit of Amsterdam in 1880 to train ministers in that same conviction. He served as Prime Minister of the Netherlands from 1901 to 1905, and in his 1898 lectures at Princeton declared that there is ‘not a square inch’ of creation over which Christ does not claim sovereignty.

Kuyper’s public brilliance was considerable, but he traced its root to a single quiet rebuke from a servant girl who cared more for his soul than his approval — a reminder that saving faith is God’s gift, not the credential of the educated.

---

## Adoniram Judson (1788–1850)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=adoniram-judson
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adoniram_Judson
Nationality: American · Tradition: Baptist · Region: Asia · Era: 19th Century
Topics: missions, suffering, perseverance, faithfulness, translation, prayer

Adoniram Judson was born in Massachusetts in 1788, the son of a minister. He sailed for Burma with his wife Ann in 1812 as one of America's first overseas missionaries, converting from Congregationalism to Baptist conviction during the voyage. His first Burmese convert was not baptised until 1819 — six years after arrival.

The Anglo-Burmese War of 1824 brought catastrophe. Judson was arrested as a suspected spy and imprisoned for seventeen months in Oung-pen-la — Death Prison — where prisoners were chained and suspended from bamboo poles at night. Ann walked to the prison daily, bribing guards for access and nursing their infant daughter Maria through the gates. Ann died in 1826 shortly after his release; Maria died weeks later.

Judson pressed on. He completed the first Burmese Bible in 1835 and compiled a Burmese-English dictionary of 25,000 entries. He lost two more wives in the field. He died at sea in 1850, having never returned to America. By then there were approximately 8,000 Burmese Christians and 100 churches.

"The future is as bright as the promises of God." A man who outlasted every loss not because he was immune to grief but because he had concluded that the gospel was owed to the Burmese regardless of what it cost him.

---

## Alexander Cruden (1699–1770)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=alexander-cruden
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alexander_Cruden
Nationality: Scottish · Tradition: Presbyterian · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 18th Century
Topics: Scripture, prayer, faithfulness, biblical scholarship

Alexander Cruden was born in Aberdeen in 1699, educated at Marischal College, and came to London in his twenties where he worked as a bookseller and proofreader. He was a committed Calvinist Presbyterian whose personal life was marked by grief, loneliness, and several breakdowns — episodes that led to his brief commitments to private asylums, events his enemies mocked and his friends mourned.

In 1737 he published the first edition of his Complete Concordance to the Holy Scriptures, having compiled it largely alone over years of painstaking work. It was the first major concordance of the entire English Bible: every significant word indexed by chapter and verse, so that any reader could locate any passage by a word they remembered. He dedicated it to Queen Caroline. He revised and expanded it in 1761 and 1769; the final edition remains in print.

In his later years he styled himself "Alexander the Corrector" and mounted a self-appointed campaign for national moral reform. He was eccentric, sometimes erratic — but he was earnest. He prayed over every page of his Concordance and regarded his life's work as a gift to the church.

His Concordance outlasted his eccentricities. For nearly three centuries it has been placing the words of Scripture within reach of those who need to find them.

---

## Alexander Whyte (1836–1921)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=alexander-whyte
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alexander_Whyte
Nationality: Scottish · Tradition: Free Church of Scotland / Presbyterian · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 19th Century
Topics: grace, sin and grace, perseverance, preaching

Alexander Whyte was born in 1836 in Kirriemuir, Scotland, to an unmarried mother, and grew up in real poverty — at twelve he was apprenticed to a shoemaker, with no obvious path to further education. A local minister, recognizing his mind, taught him Latin and Greek in his spare hours, opening the way to study at Aberdeen and New College, Edinburgh.

Ordained in the Free Church of Scotland, Whyte began ministry at Free St John's, Glasgow, in 1866, then in 1870 became colleague and successor to Robert Candlish at Free St George's, Edinburgh, where he preached for nearly forty years. He became known for sermons that named sin plainly and specifically — refusing the comfortable vagueness many preachers use — precisely so that grace could be named just as specifically. His studies of John Bunyan's allegories, published as Bunyan Characters, trace this same conviction: that the gospel means little to people who have never been shown, in detail, what they need saving from.

A man raised with every social disadvantage his century could name went on to lead one of Edinburgh's most influential pulpits, later training its next generation of ministers as Principal of New College. Whyte never let his own unlikely rise become the point of the story — his sermons kept turning back to one claim: that grace runs deepest exactly where a person's own record gives them least to boast about. He died in 1921.

---

## Allen Gardiner (1794–c. 1851)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=allen-gardiner
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Allen_Francis_Gardiner
Nationality: English · Tradition: Church of England (evangelical) · Region: Latin America · Era: 19th Century
Topics: missions, perseverance, sacrifice, faithfulness, suffering

Allen Francis Gardiner spent much of his early life as a Royal Navy officer, serving with distinction in the War of 1812. The death of his first wife in 1834 redirected him toward missions: he founded a short-lived station among the Zulu in southern Africa, then turned to South America, working among Chile's indigenous peoples and making his first landing on Tierra del Fuego in 1842. Established missionary societies repeatedly declined to fund so remote and dangerous a field, so in 1844 Gardiner founded his own — the Patagonian Missionary Society, later renamed the South American Missionary Society.

Two further attempts to establish a Tierra del Fuego mission failed. In September 1850, at fifty-six, Gardiner sailed once more, with six companions, for a final attempt. Landing at Picton Island, they found the Yaghan people hostile and relocated to Spaniard Harbour — but most of their ammunition had been left aboard ship, leaving them unable to hunt, and the relief ship never came in time. Over the following months, all seven starved; Gardiner, the last survivor, is believed to have died around 6 September 1851.

When a search ship found the camp, searchers discovered verses from Psalm 62 still painted on the rocks nearby: 'Trust in him at all times... God is a refuge for us.' Gardiner's death, rather than ending the mission, became the very thing that funded and inspired its eventual success — a field watered by grace that outlasted his own strength.

---

## Amy Carmichael (1867–1951)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=amy-carmichael
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amy_Carmichael
Nationality: Irish · Tradition: Protestant evangelical · Region: Asia · Era: 20th Century
Topics: missions, suffering, faithfulness, perseverance

Amy Carmichael was born in 1867 in Millisle, County Down. She came to faith as a child and as a young woman worked in mission among mill girls in Belfast and Manchester.

She arrived in India in 1895 and settled in Dohnavur in Tamil Nadu, where she would spend fifty-five years — never returning to Britain. She founded the Dohnavur Fellowship to shelter girls dedicated to Hindu temples for religious prostitution, putting her in direct conflict with entrenched custom.

In 1931 a serious fall left her partly disabled; for her last twenty years she was largely confined to her room, yet wrote more than thirty books and continued to lead the community.

She died in Dohnavur in 1951. Her grave has no headstone — only a birdbath placed there by the children she had rescued. She had asked for no memorial.

---

## Andraé Crouch (1942–2015)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=andrae-crouch
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andra%C3%A9_Crouch
Nationality: American · Tradition: Pentecostal (Church of God in Christ) · Region: North America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: worship, grace, gospel music, healing
Hymns: Soon and Very Soon, My Tribute (To God Be the Glory), The Blood Will Never Lose Its Power

Andraé Crouch was born in 1942 in Los Angeles, a twin to his sister Sandra, the son of Benjamin Crouch, a Church of God in Christ minister who pastored a small congregation in Pacoima while running a dry-cleaning business to support his family. Andraé began playing piano in church services at eleven and had written his first gospel song by fourteen.

Through his group The Disciples, and later as a solo artist and producer, Crouch pioneered a style that folded soul, jazz, and pop into gospel music — work that helped create contemporary Christian music as a genre and earned him seven Grammy Awards and an Oscar nomination. Songs like “My Tribute (To God Be the Glory),” “The Blood Will Never Lose Its Power,” and “Soon and Very Soon” moved beyond church walls into hymnals and radio across denominations and continents.

After his father's death, Crouch and Sandra returned to pastor the church that had raised them. He endured four bouts of cancer and, in his final years, congestive heart failure, dying in January 2015. His sister's parting words captured the theology behind every song he wrote: he had “gone home to be with the Lord.” Crouch never treated his gifts as his own achievement — his music consistently pointed past his own considerable talent to the God who alone deserved the tribute, the glory, and the credit for any good in it.

---

## Andrew Fuller (1754–1815)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=andrew-fuller
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andrew_Fuller
Nationality: English · Tradition: Particular Baptist · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 18th Century
Topics: grace, missions, gospel, faith, perseverance

Andrew Fuller was born in 1754 in Wicken, Cambridgeshire, England, into a farming family within the Particular Baptist tradition. Converted and called to preach as a young man, he was ordained pastor of Soham Baptist Church in 1775, and from 1782 served the church at Kettering, Northamptonshire, until his death.

Fuller grew up under the shadow of 'hyper-Calvinism' — a teaching, common among Particular Baptists of his day, that reasoned no one should be urged to believe the gospel unless they first showed signs of being among God's elect. Fuller found this crippling, both in his own soul and in his preaching, and in 1785 published The Gospel Worthy of All Acceptation, arguing from Scripture that every sinner, without exception, is commanded and welcome to believe — because salvation is offered by grace, through faith, and not earned by any prior qualification.

That conviction bore fruit directly: in 1792, Fuller helped found the Baptist Missionary Society at Kettering, and became its first secretary, tirelessly raising support so that his friend William Carey could sail for India as its first missionary.

Fuller buried eight of his eleven children and his first wife, and was worn down by decades of unpaid, exhausting labour for the Society. He died in 1815, having spent his life insisting that the gospel is genuinely good news — for everyone who will simply believe it.

---

## Anthony Norris Groves (1795–1853)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=anthony-norris-groves
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anthony_Norris_Groves
Nationality: English · Tradition: Plymouth Brethren · Region: Asia · Era: 19th Century
Topics: missions, faith, sacrifice, stewardship, perseverance

Anthony Norris Groves (1795–1853) was an English dentist with a thriving practice who, in his late twenties, began to ask what total surrender to Christ meant in practice. In 1825 he wrote Christian Devotedness, arguing from the Sermon on the Mount that Christians should hold wealth loosely and trust God for provision. His brother-in-law George Müller read it, and it reshaped the direction of his life.

Groves gave up his dental practice and offered himself to the Church Missionary Society for Baghdad, but concluded he could not in good conscience be ordained under any denomination's authority. He went anyway — one of the first independent faith missionaries in Protestant history. In Baghdad from 1829, he faced catastrophe: plague, war, and famine. His wife Mary died in 1831; two of his children died. He then worked in India for a further seventeen years until his death in 1853.

Groves is called the father of faith missions — not for the scale of his results, but for what he demonstrated: that it was possible to go without institutional salary, trusting God for daily provision. Hudson Taylor, a generation later, shaped the China Inland Mission on this principle. Groves's life was shadowed by loss. He did not interpret that as God's desertion. He had cast himself entirely on God — and the casting did not make suffering lighter, but it made it bearable.

---

## Augustus Toplady (1740–1778)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=augustus-toplady
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Augustus_Toplady
Nationality: English · Tradition: Anglican evangelical (Calvinist) · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 18th Century
Topics: grace, redemption, gospel, cross
Hymns: Rock of Ages

Augustus Toplady was born in Farnham, Surrey in 1740. As a teenager in Ireland, he heard an uneducated lay preacher speak from Ephesians 2:13 in a barn, and the gospel of free grace became real to him.

A convinced Calvinist, he engaged in sharp controversy with John Wesley — sometimes intemperate in his polemics. But beneath the controversy lay a genuine conviction: salvation is entirely God's work, and any human contribution to it corrupts the gospel.

He is remembered not for controversies but for one hymn written in 1776: Rock of Ages. It is the dissolution of every form of self-justification: "Not the labours of my hands can fulfil thy law's demands... thou must save, and thou alone." Nothing is brought but need; nothing is received but Christ.

Toplandy died of tuberculosis at thirty-seven, leaving a single hymn sung for nearly 250 years.

---

## Bakht Singh (1903–2000)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=bakht-singh
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bakht_Singh
Nationality: Indian · Tradition: Independent evangelical · Region: Asia · Era: 20th Century
Topics: missions, revival, church planting, prayer, discipleship

Bakht Singh was born in 1903 into a Sikh family in Punjab, India. He left for England to study engineering, then moved to Canada. Bitter, restless, and far from home, he was given a New Testament by a stranger in Winnipeg in 1929 and was converted after reading it through the night.

He returned to India and began preaching in a country resistant to Christianity as a colonial religion. He founded assemblies on simple New Testament principles — no Western denominational structures, no paid clergy, daily prayer, breaking of bread, and Scripture. He is estimated to have planted more than two hundred churches across India, Sri Lanka, and beyond.

His preaching drew vast crowds — thousands gathering across south India for conventions at which he would preach for hours. He was known for fasting, long night-watches of prayer, and direct, uncompromising proclamation of the cross.

He died in 2000, aged 96, still active in ministry. He had sought to show that Christianity was not the white man's religion imported from the West, but the faith of a risen Lord who belonged to every nation — including India.

---

## Betty Stam (1906–1934)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=betty-stam
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_and_Betty_Stam
Nationality: American · Tradition: Evangelical · Region: Asia · Era: 20th Century
Topics: martyrdom, missions, sacrifice, courage, faithfulness

Betty Scott Stam was born in 1906 in Tsingtao (Qingdao), China, where her parents served as missionaries. She grew up bilingual in English and Chinese, graduated from Wilson College in Pennsylvania, and sailed back to China with the China Inland Mission in 1931. She married John Stam in 1933.

She had made her commitment clear before she went. As a student she had written: "Lord, I give up all my own plans and purposes, all my own desires and hopes, and accept Thy will for my life." Her letters from China showed a woman of steady faith, warm humour, and clear theological depth.

In December 1934 she and John were captured by Communist soldiers in Anhui province with their two-month-old daughter Helen Priscilla. On 8 December they were beheaded. Their baby was found alive.

Betty was 28. She and John had served in China for little more than a year as a married couple. Yet the account of their death, published immediately and distributed worldwide, became one of the most powerful missionary testimonies of the twentieth century — not because it ended in victory by human measure, but because it showed two young lives held loosely, offered freely, and not found wanting when the moment came.

---

## Billy Graham (1918–2018)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=billy-graham
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Billy_Graham
Nationality: American · Tradition: Southern Baptist evangelical · Region: North America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: evangelism, revival, grace, conversion, preaching, mission
Note: In a 1997 televised interview with Robert Schuller, Graham suggested that people who have never explicitly heard of or believed in Christ might nonetheless be saved through him unknowingly — an inclusivist remark that drew criticism from conservative evangelicals for softening the New Testament's call to explicit, conscious faith in Christ. It stands apart from his lifelong preaching, which consistently proclaimed a grace-alone gospel and called hearers to a personal, explicit decision for Christ. He was also criticized by some fundamentalist Protestants for cooperating with Roman Catholic and mainline clergy on local crusade organizing committees, a practice he defended as sending converts back to whatever church they already belonged to rather than building his own following.

William Franklin Graham Jr. was born in 1918 on a dairy farm near Charlotte, North Carolina, and raised in a Presbyterian church. At sixteen he was converted at revival meetings led by evangelist Mordecai Ham, later trained at Wheaton College, and was ordained a Southern Baptist minister in 1939.

In 1948 Graham and his team drew up the Modesto Manifesto, a code of financial and personal integrity that became a model across evangelistic ministry. His 1949 Los Angeles tent crusade made him a national figure, and in 1950 he founded the Billy Graham Evangelistic Association, through which he pioneered evangelism by radio, television, and film, and helped launch Christianity Today magazine. Over six decades of crusades he preached the gospel face to face to more people than anyone before him, continuing the altar call practice begun by Dwight L. Moody, and always closing with Charlotte Elliott's hymn "Just As I Am."

From the 1950s Graham insisted his crusades be racially integrated at a time when much of America was not. At a 1953 rally in Chattanooga, Tennessee, he tore down the ropes organizers had strung to separate the races, telling ushers to leave them down "or you can go on and have the revival without me." For Graham it was simply the gospel worked out: salvation is a gift held out to anyone who will receive it by faith, without regard to race or merit, because Christ's death paid a debt none of us could pay ourselves.

---

## Billy Sunday (1862–1935)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=billy-sunday
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Billy_Sunday
Nationality: American · Tradition: Presbyterian evangelical · Region: North America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: evangelism, conversion, revival, gospel, grace
Note: At several campaign meetings in the 1920s, Sunday accepted public support and financial gifts from Ku Klux Klan members, and his ministry associate publicly praised the Klan on at least one occasion; Sunday is also on record using dehumanizing language toward striking coal miners. These stand in real tension with the gospel he preached and are recorded here plainly, alongside his evangelistic legacy, not to condemn the man but to give a full and honest picture.

Billy Sunday was born in 1862 near Ames, Iowa, and grew up to become a fast, popular outfielder in Major League Baseball during the 1880s. Around 1886–87, invited into Chicago's Pacific Garden Mission by hymns he remembered from childhood, Sunday committed his life to Christ. In 1891 he turned down a lucrative baseball contract to take a modest job with the YMCA instead.

After several years learning the evangelist's trade as an advance man for preacher J. Wilbur Chapman, Sunday began holding his own campaigns in 1896. His preaching was unlike anything American congregations had seen — athletic, plain-spoken, delivered at a run, often ending with Sunday sliding across the stage as if stealing a base. Over the following decades he preached an estimated twenty thousand sermons to more than one hundred million people, with vast crowds coming forward across the sawdust-covered floors of his temporary wooden tabernacles — an experience that gave rise to the phrase 'hitting the sawdust trail.'

Sunday's central message never changed, however plain its delivery: that no one, however respectable or however far gone, is saved by their own effort, but only by turning from sin to trust in Christ. Days before his death in 1935, his final sermon still asked the same question he had spent his life answering: 'What must I do to be saved?' His answer, then as always, was simply — believe.

---

## Bilquis Sheikh (c. 1919–1997)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=bilquis-sheikh
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bilquis_Sheikh
Nationality: Pakistani · Tradition: Independent evangelical · Region: Asia · Era: 20th Century
Topics: grace, conversion, persecution, faithfulness, gospel
Note: Sheikh's account of her conversion includes a series of dreams and visions that she took as confirmation from God, alongside her reading of Scripture. As with other reported mystical experiences on this site, these are recounted as she described them but are unverifiable, and readers are invited to weigh them with appropriate discernment.

Bilquis Sheikh was born into a prominent Muslim family near Rawalpindi in Punjab, in what is now Pakistan; her husband, General Khalid Masud Sheikh, later served as the country's Interior Minister. Comfortable and well-connected, she nonetheless found herself searching for God beyond the religious duties she had always kept. She asked her Christian chauffeur to bring her a Bible, and reading it privately, she was struck by its picture of God as a Father who could be known personally rather than only obeyed at a distance.

Through contact with missionaries David and Synnøve Mitchell, and after a period of prayer, searching, and dreams she took as confirmation from God, Sheikh committed her life to Christ on 24 December 1966. In the culture she belonged to, the baptism of a Muslim convert was treated as a decisive and public act, and when news of her conversion spread, her own family shunned her as a traitor. She received threats, and her home was eventually burned.

Sheikh fled Pakistan for the United States in 1972, where she spent years speaking in churches and at Billy Graham Crusades about what she had found. Her 1978 memoir, I Dared to Call Him Father, told the story of a woman who had kept every religious rule she knew and still felt distant from God — until she discovered she could simply call him Father, not because she had earned it, but because Christ had made a way for her to be received as a daughter.

---

## Brother Andrew (1928–2022)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=brother-andrew
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brother_Andrew
Nationality: Dutch · Tradition: Interdenominational evangelical · Region: Europe · Era: 20th Century
Topics: missions, faithfulness, persecution, prayer, gospel

Andrew van der Bijl was born in Sint Pancras, Netherlands in 1928 to a poor family. After a restless youth and a stint in the Dutch colonial army in Indonesia, he came to faith through a missionary tract and trained at a missionary college in Glasgow.

In 1955 he attended a communist youth festival in Warsaw and saw the reality of the persecuted church behind the Iron Curtain. He returned home determined to act. Over the following years he made repeated journeys into Poland, East Germany, Czechoslovakia, Hungary, Yugoslavia, Bulgaria, and the Soviet Union, carrying Bibles hidden in a battered Volkswagen. At every border crossing he prayed: "Lord, you made blind eyes see. Now make seeing eyes blind — do not let the guards see what you do not want them to see." The Bibles crossed borders unseen.

He founded Open Doors in 1955 to support persecuted Christians worldwide, an organisation that today serves believers in over sixty countries. In later years he turned to the Middle East, meeting Hamas and Hezbollah leaders in order to bring the gospel.

He died in Harderwijk in September 2022, aged ninety-four. He wrote: "I believe in the power of the Bible more than any other weapon on earth." A man who spent his life putting God's Word into the hands of those who were dying to have it.

---

## C. T. Studd (1860–1931)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=ct-studd
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/C._T._Studd
Nationality: British · Tradition: Interdenominational evangelical · Region: Africa · Era: 19th Century
Topics: sacrifice, missions, faith, commitment, perseverance

Charles Thomas Studd was born in 1860, the son of a wealthy Huntingdonshire landowner recently converted to evangelical faith. Studd became one of England’s finest cricketers — a Cambridge blue and England international — and at twenty-one, entirely unconcerned about God.

The turning point came through D. L. Moody’s Cambridge mission in 1882. Studd was converted, and within three years had given his entire inheritance — £29,000 — to various Christian causes and sailed for China with the “Cambridge Seven” alongside Hudson Taylor’s China Inland Mission in 1885.

After six years in China he served in India (1900–1906) and then, at age fifty-two and already suffering from asthma, went to Africa. In 1913 he founded the Heart of Africa Mission — later WEC International — and spent his final sixteen years in the Belgian Congo. He died at Ibambi in July 1931.

He was not a simple man. He drove himself and others hard. But the conviction that drove him was theological, not temperamental: “If Jesus Christ be God and died for me, then no sacrifice can be too great for me to make for Him.”

Three continents. Total obedience. Everything poured out. He did not consider it heroism — he considered it the only logical response to a Saviour who had given everything.

---

## C.S. Lewis (1898–1963)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=cs-lewis
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/C._S._Lewis
Nationality: Irish / British · Tradition: Anglican · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 20th Century
Topics: gospel, grace, conversion, faithfulness
Note: Lewis held some Anglo-Catholic views including belief in purgatory, not shared by all evangelical believers. His writings should be read with discernment on these points, though his core apologetic and gospel witness is broadly orthodox.

Clive Staples Lewis was born in Belfast in 1898. His mother died when he was nine. He became a committed atheist — committed enough to argue seriously against belief and to regard Christianity as wishful thinking dressed up as logic.

His conversion was gradual and intellectual. He found the atheist position required too much explaining away — particularly the persistent human longing for something beyond the material world. He became a theist in 1929, and a Christian in 1931, in part through sustained conversation with J.R.R. Tolkien.

Mere Christianity presented a reasoned case for belief to educated sceptics. The Screwtape Letters explored the Christian life through satirical inversion. The Chronicles of Narnia communicated the shape of the gospel through fiction. Millions have found their way to faith through his work.

Note: Lewis held some Anglo-Catholic views, including belief in purgatory, not shared by most Protestants.

---

## Charles Albert Tindley (1851–1933)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=charles-albert-tindley
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charles_Albert_Tindley
Nationality: American · Tradition: Methodist Episcopal · Region: North America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: gospel music, perseverance, grace, civil rights
Hymns: I'll Overcome Someday, Stand By Me, Take Your Burden to the Lord and Leave It There, What Are They Doing in Heaven?

Charles Albert Tindley was born in 1851 in Berlin, Maryland, the son of an enslaved father, yet born free himself. After the Civil War he moved to Philadelphia, working as a hod carrier and church sexton while teaching himself to read from discarded scraps of paper. He later mastered Hebrew and Greek through independent and correspondence study and was ordained in the Methodist Episcopal Church, becoming an elder in 1889.

In 1906 he returned to the small congregation where he had once swept floors, East Calvary Methodist Episcopal Church, as its pastor. Under his preaching it grew from 130 members to more than 10,000 — Black and white worshipping side by side in a city still divided by race. He is remembered today as the father of American gospel music, writing hymns that turned hard-won faith into song: “Stand By Me,” “What Are They Doing in Heaven?,” and “Take Your Burden to the Lord and Leave It There.”

His hymn “I'll Overcome Someday” later gave its melody and message to the Civil Rights anthem “We Shall Overcome.” But Tindley's “someday” was never a slogan first — it was the hope of a former sexton who knew that no burden, however heavy, could out-hope the grace that carries the weary all the way home. He did not write to be remembered; he wrote so that suffering people would keep singing of Christ until their burdens were finally, fully lifted.

---

## Charles Haddon Spurgeon (1834–1892)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=charles-spurgeon
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charles_Spurgeon
Nationality: British · Tradition: Particular Baptist · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 19th Century
Topics: preaching, grace, evangelism, suffering, cross, faith

Charles Haddon Spurgeon was born in Kelvedon, Essex in 1834 and converted to faith in Christ at fifteen. During a snowstorm he ducked into a Primitive Methodist chapel in Colchester, where a stand-in preacher addressed the congregation on Isaiah 45:22 — “Look unto me, and be ye saved.” The words struck Spurgeon like a lance, and he looked, and he was saved.

By nineteen he was called to New Park Street Chapel, London. The congregation outgrew every building they could find; by 1861 the Metropolitan Tabernacle had been built, seating six thousand. He preached to an estimated ten million people in person over his lifetime. He founded a Pastors’ College, an orphanage, and wrote prolifically — his sermons filling sixty-three volumes.

He battled depression and gout for much of his life and was candid about it, writing that he had learned more of God’s grace through suffering than through any other school. In 1887 he withdrew from the Baptist Union over its drift toward theological liberalism, at considerable personal cost.

He died in Menton, France in January 1892. His message over forty years never changed: all are lost, Christ alone saves, and the grace that saves a sinner is the grace that keeps him to the end. He was not the message’s author — only its most tireless herald.

---

## Charles Simeon (1759–1836)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=charles-simeon
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charles_Simeon
Nationality: English · Tradition: Anglican evangelical · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 18th Century
Topics: perseverance, preaching, missions, conversion, pastoral ministry

Charles Simeon was born in Reading in 1759 and came to Cambridge as a wealthy, worldly undergraduate at King's College. Ordered to attend communion, and dreading it, he prepared by reading a book on the Lord's Supper — and there, in 1779, understood for the first time that Christ himself had borne his sins as a substitute. “I must lay my sins on the head of my substitute,” he later wrote of that moment.

In 1782, aged just 23, he was appointed vicar of Holy Trinity Church, Cambridge — a post he held for fifty-four years. His own congregation despised him at first: churchwardens locked the pews against him and threw out benches he brought in for the poor, and hostility toward him lingered for years. He answered by starting an extra service for those the church excluded, and simply stayed. Over decades he became one of the most trusted trainers of evangelical clergy in England, mentoring young ministers such as Henry Martyn, and helped found the Church Missionary Society in 1799.

Simeon never married and gave away much of his income, living simply to the end. He died in 1836, still vicar of the same church, having outlasted every one of his early opponents — proof, he would have said, not of his own persistence but of a Savior who does not let go of those he has bought.

---

## Charles Wesley (1707–1788)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=charles-wesley
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charles_Wesley
Nationality: English · Tradition: Methodist · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 18th Century
Topics: grace, conversion, praise, worship, gospel
Hymns: Love Divine All Loves Excelling, Christ the Lord Is Risen Today, Amazing Love, Come, Thou Long-Expected Jesus, God, Our Father, We Adore You, Hark, the Herald Angels Sing, Oh, for a Thousand Tongues to Sing, Rejoice, the Lord Is King

Charles Wesley was born in Epworth, Lincolnshire in 1707, the eighteenth of nineteen children. At Oxford he co-founded the Holy Club with his brother John — disciplined and earnest, but not yet living faith.

In May 1738, three days before John's famous Aldersgate experience, Charles received assurance that Christ had died for him personally, that his sins were forgiven, that he was accepted before God not by his efforts but by grace. He wrote the next morning: And Can It Be? — with its astonishing question: "And can it be that I should gain an interest in the Saviour's blood?"

He wrote more than 6,000 hymns — a body of verse without rival in Christian history. Among the best loved are O for a Thousand Tongues to Sing, Hark the Herald Angels Sing, Love Divine All Loves Excelling, and Christ the Lord Is Risen Today. These are doctrinal statements in verse: the incarnation, the atonement, the resurrection, expressed in words congregations can sing and remember.

---

## Charlotte Elliott (1789–1871)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=charlotte-elliott
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charlotte_Elliott
Nationality: English · Tradition: Anglican evangelical · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 19th Century
Topics: hymns, grace, suffering, perseverance, faith
Hymns: Just As I Am, Without One Plea, My God and Father While I Stray

Charlotte Elliott was born in Brighton in 1789, granddaughter of Henry Venn of the Clapham Sect and raised among evangelical believers. For her first thirty years she lived a cultured but spiritually empty life. A serious illness in 1821 unsettled her, and in May 1822 a Swiss evangelist named César Malan visited her father's home and asked whether she had made her peace with God. Elliott resented the question — but it would not leave her. Days later she sought Malan out. When she asked how to come to Christ, he said simply: “Come just as you are.” She trusted Christ that day, marking 9 May 1822 as the birthday of her soul.

From 1829 Charlotte suffered chronic illness that largely confined her to her room for decades. She might have become bitter; instead she edited The Invalid's Hymn Book and wrote some 150 hymns — most anonymously — convinced that a bedridden saint could still serve Christ through words. In 1836 she wrote ‘Just As I Am, Without One Plea.’ Its stanzas strip away every human condition for approaching God and rest the soul entirely on the blood of Christ. Hymnologist Kenneth Osbeck called it the hymn that had “touched more hearts and influenced more people for Christ than any other song.” Billy Graham used it at virtually every evangelistic crusade he ever led.

Charlotte Elliott brought nothing to God but her need. That was exactly what the gospel had always asked.

---

## Christmas Evans (1766–1838)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=christmas-evans
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Christmas_Evans
Nationality: Welsh · Tradition: Baptist · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 18th Century
Topics: preaching, perseverance, conversion, evangelism

Christmas Evans was born on Christmas Day, 1766, near Llandysul in Cardiganshire, Wales — the son the day itself named. His father, a shoemaker, died while Evans was still a child, and he grew up illiterate and desperately poor, working as a farm labourer. As a young man he lost an eye in a brawl, a disfigurement he carried the rest of his life.

At seventeen he went to work for a Presbyterian minister, David Davies, and during a season of religious revival there learned to read, in both Welsh and English, for the first time. He joined the Baptists around 1789 and became an itinerant preacher across Wales, eventually settling for years on Anglesey before later ministries in Caerphilly, Cardiff, and Caernarfon. His preaching became legendary for its imaginative power and physical intensity — contemporaries described sermons that held vast open-air crowds motionless for hours, delivered by a large, one-eyed man who had once had nothing, not even the ability to read the Bible he came to preach.

Evans never let his own transformation — from illiterate, disfigured farmhand to Wales's most famous preacher — become the substance of his message. What he preached, sermon after sermon for nearly fifty years, was simpler and not about himself: that Christ receives sinners exactly as ruined as he had once been, and asks nothing from them but faith. He died in 1838, still on the road, still preaching.

---

## Chuck Colson (1931–2012)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=chuck-colson
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charles_Colson
Nationality: American · Tradition: Nondenominational evangelical · Region: North America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: grace, conversion, forgiveness, redemption, prison ministry
Note: In 1994 Colson co-authored 'Evangelicals and Catholics Together' with Richard John Neuhaus, an ecumenical statement affirming shared mission with Roman Catholic leaders. The document drew significant criticism from Reformed and other evangelicals for understating the doctrinal differences — particularly over justification by faith alone — that divide evangelical and Catholic teaching. Colson personally continued to affirm salvation by grace through faith alone throughout his life; this is noted so readers can weigh the ecumenical judgment call alongside his testimony.

Charles 'Chuck' Colson was born in 1931 in Boston, Massachusetts, and rose to become Special Counsel to President Richard Nixon from 1969 to 1973, earning a reputation as Nixon's toughest and most ruthless aide — his self-described 'hatchet man.'

When the Watergate scandal broke, Colson was named among the 'Watergate Seven' and faced prosecution. Shortly before he was due to appear in court, a friend gave him a copy of C. S. Lewis's Mere Christianity. Reading it, Colson broke down and gave his life to Christ. In June 1974 he pleaded guilty to obstruction of justice and served seven months in federal prison. His conversion was so unexpected that commentators later remarked it was proof Watergate had really happened — no one, they said, could have invented a story in which even Colson turned to Christ.

After his release, Colson founded Prison Fellowship in 1976, and it grew into the world's largest ministry to prisoners and their families. He spent the rest of his life writing, broadcasting, and advocating for those the world had written off — the same grace that had reached him in disgrace, offered freely to men and women in cells much like the one he had occupied. Colson was always careful to say that nothing he built afterward had earned his forgiveness; it had only followed it.

---

## Corrie ten Boom (1892–1983)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=corrie-ten-boom
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Corrie_ten_Boom
Nationality: Dutch · Tradition: Dutch Reformed evangelical · Region: Europe · Era: 20th Century
Topics: persecution, forgiveness, suffering, faithfulness, grace

Corrie ten Boom was born in Haarlem, Netherlands in 1892, the daughter of a watchmaker. The ten Boom family were devout Dutch Reformed Christians and their home above the shop was a centre of Christian community for decades.

When the Nazis occupied Holland, the family began sheltering Jewish people in a secret room built behind a false wall in Corrie's bedroom. In February 1944 they were betrayed and arrested. Corrie and her sister Betsie were sent to Ravensbrück concentration camp in Germany. Betsie died there in December 1944. Corrie was released the same week — through a clerical error — one week before all women of her age in the camp were executed.

After the war, Corrie spent thirty years travelling the world speaking about forgiveness. In 1947, at a church in Munich, she came face to face with one of the SS guards who had mocked them at Ravensbrück. He extended his hand. She prayed, reached out, and later wrote: "The current started in my shoulder, raced down my arm, sprang into our joined hands. And then this healing warmth seemed to flood my whole being... I forgive you, brother! With all my heart." She had, she said, never felt God's love so intensely as in that moment.

---

## César Malan (1787–1864)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=cesar-malan
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/César_Malan
Nationality: Swiss · Tradition: Reformed (Calvinist) · Region: Europe · Era: 19th Century
Topics: grace, gospel, perseverance, faithfulness, suffering

César Malan was born in Geneva in 1787 and ordained as a Reformed minister in 1810. His early preaching was conventional, but in 1816 Scottish evangelist Robert Haldane came to Geneva and taught a group of young ministers through the letter to the Romans. Malan emerged from those studies gripped by justification by faith alone — and could not stop preaching it.

In 1817 a sermon on the new birth brought his first suspension. Restored, he preached the same again and was formally defrocked in 1823. He responded by founding l'Église du témoignage — the Church of Testimony — in his own garden, where he preached for forty years. He made nine journeys to Britain and was known for personal conversations about the soul's need of Christ.

One such conversation, in May 1822, changed English hymnody. At a dinner in Brighton, Malan spoke of being useful to God to a young woman named Charlotte Elliott. She resented the remark. Days later she sought him out and asked how she might come to Christ. He told her simply: “Come just as you are.” She trusted Christ that day. Fourteen years later she turned those words into the hymn ‘Just As I Am, Without One Plea.’

Malan was defrocked for preaching that salvation is by faith alone. The words he spoke to Charlotte Elliott proved him right.

---

## Daniel W. Whittle (1840–1901)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=daniel-w-whittle
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Daniel_W._Whittle
Nationality: American · Tradition: Evangelical (Moody circle) · Region: North America · Era: 19th Century
Topics: gospel, grace, perseverance, faithfulness, assurance
Hymns: Christ Liveth in Me, Have You Any Room for Jesus?, I Know Whom I Have Believed, Moment by Moment

Daniel Webster Whittle (1840–1901) was born in Chicopee Falls, Massachusetts. He served in the Union Army during the Civil War, was wounded and taken prisoner, and during his recovery read a New Testament his mother had placed in his kit. He came to faith in Christ in those weeks — a soldier's conversion, in pain, in a prison, with no certainty about what came next.

After the war he worked at the Elgin Watch Company, then in 1873, under the influence of D.L. Moody, entered full-time evangelistic work. He partnered with Philip P. Bliss — who set his texts to music — and later with James McGranahan, conducting revival campaigns across the United States and Great Britain. His hymns include I Know Whom I Have Believed, Moment by Moment, and Christ Liveth in Me.

I Know Whom I Have Believed is built on 2 Timothy 1:12. It does not claim to understand everything — "I know not why God's wondrous grace / To me He hath made known" — but asserts a knowledge that counts for everything: I know Christ, and He will keep what I have entrusted to Him until the last day. Whittle's answer to doubt was not a solved argument but a known Person. A converted soldier who became an evangelist, he spent his ministry helping others understand that faith is trust in a Person whose faithfulness makes our stumbling commitment secure.

---

## David Brainerd (1718–1747)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=david-brainerd
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/David_Brainerd
Nationality: American · Tradition: Presbyterian · Region: North America · Era: 18th Century
Topics: missions, prayer, perseverance, suffering, faithfulness

David Brainerd was born in 1718 in Haddam, Connecticut, orphaned at thirteen, and entered Yale College in 1739 during the First Great Awakening. He was expelled in 1742 for a remark made in frustration about a tutor — a wound that barred him from licensed ministry and that he carried for the rest of his short life.

He became instead a missionary to the Delaware and Mahican peoples, appointed by the Society in Scotland for Propagating Christian Knowledge. He laboured in New York, Pennsylvania, and New Jersey — often in poverty, always ill with tuberculosis. He spent long nights in prayer in the forests, sometimes in such spiritual anguish that the snow around him melted.

In 1745 a remarkable revival broke out at Crossweeksung, New Jersey. Within months nearly a hundred Native Americans had come to faith. Brainerd preached through an interpreter but believed it was prayer, not his words, that opened hearts.

He died in October 1747, aged twenty-nine, at the home of Jonathan Edwards in Northampton, Massachusetts. Edwards edited his diary and published it the following year. That diary has moved readers ever since — most significantly William Carey and Jim Elliot, who both counted Brainerd’s example among their chief reasons for going to the mission field.

He accomplished little the world counts great. He was spent for Christ, and that proved enough.

---

## David Livingstone (1813–1873)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=david-livingstone
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/David_Livingstone
Nationality: Scottish · Tradition: Congregationalist / London Missionary Society · Region: Africa · Era: 19th Century
Topics: missions, faithfulness, perseverance, suffering

David Livingstone was born in Blantyre, Lanarkshire in 1813. He worked in a cotton mill from the age of ten and educated himself by propping books against the machinery. He qualified as a physician and sailed for southern Africa with the London Missionary Society in 1840.

His explorations — across the Kalahari Desert, along the Zambezi River — made him the most celebrated explorer of the Victorian era. But exploration was never his primary purpose: he was a missionary who explored, driven by the conviction that the interior of Africa could be opened to legitimate trade and the gospel as alternatives to the slave trade.

He died on 1 May 1873 at Chitambo's village in what is now Zambia, found kneeling by his bedside — he had died in the night, apparently in prayer. His servants carried his embalmed body over a thousand miles to the coast.

---

## Dietrich Bonhoeffer (1906–1945)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=dietrich-bonhoeffer
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dietrich_Bonhoeffer
Nationality: German · Tradition: Lutheran · Region: Europe · Era: 20th Century
Topics: suffering, martyrdom, discipleship, courage, persecution
Hymns: By Gracious Powers
Note: Bonhoeffer's later prison writings (Letters and Papers from Prison) use language — 'religionless Christianity,' 'the world come of age' — that liberal theologians have interpreted in ways departing from orthodox Christianity. Most evangelical scholars regard his core convictions, especially as expressed in The Cost of Discipleship and Life Together, as soundly orthodox. Readers should approach his later writings with discernment.

Dietrich Bonhoeffer was born in Breslau in 1906, the son of a psychiatrist. He studied theology in Berlin and at Union Theological Seminary in New York, and was ordained as a Lutheran pastor. When Hitler came to power in 1933, Bonhoeffer immediately recognised the threat and joined the Confessing Church — the movement within German Protestantism that resisted the Nazi-aligned "German Christians."

He directed an illegal seminary at Finkenwalde from 1935 until the Gestapo closed it in 1937. His books The Cost of Discipleship and Life Together were written in this period. In them he argued that "cheap grace" — forgiveness without repentance, faith without obedience — was a corruption of the gospel; true discipleship meant taking up the cross in the concrete circumstances of one's life.

He was arrested in April 1943 and executed at Flossenbürg concentration camp on 9 April 1945, three weeks before Germany's surrender. The camp doctor who witnessed his death wrote that he had never seen anyone die so entirely submissive to the will of God.

"When Christ calls a man," he wrote, "he bids him come and die."

---

## Dottie Rambo (1934–2008)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=dottie-rambo
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dottie_Rambo
Nationality: American · Tradition: Evangelical (Southern Gospel) · Region: North America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: grace, suffering, forgiveness, gospel music
Hymns: We Shall Behold Him, He Looked Beyond My Fault and Saw My Need, I Go to the Rock

Dottie Rambo was born Joyce Reba Luttrell in 1934 in Madisonville, Kentucky, into a poor family. At twelve she committed her life to Christ against her father's wishes and left home to pursue gospel music. At sixteen she married Buck Rambo at a revival meeting, and together with their daughter Reba they formed The Singing Rambos, touring for decades — including a 1967 tour singing for American troops in Vietnam aboard the carriers USS Kitty Hawk and USS Ticonderoga.

Rambo wrote more than 2,500 songs across her lifetime, among them “We Shall Behold Him,” “I Go to the Rock,” and “He Looked Beyond My Fault and Saw My Need” — a hymn whose title states the gospel outright: that Christ does not wait for us to become worthy before loving us. A ruptured disk left her temporarily paralyzed in 1987, and her marriage ended in divorce in 1994, but she kept writing until her final years, releasing new work in her sixties after a long silence.

She died in 2008 in a tour bus accident on her way to a Mother's Day concert. At her funeral, a flag that had flown over the White House was presented in her honor — a public tribute to a woman whose songs had already told the truer story: that whatever a person's fault, God looks beyond it to the need beneath, and meets that need in Christ.

---

## Dwight L. Moody (1837–1899)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=dwight-l-moody
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dwight_L._Moody
Nationality: American · Tradition: Non-denominational evangelical · Region: North America · Era: 19th Century
Topics: evangelism, revival, grace, conversion, prayer, missions

Dwight Lyman Moody was born in 1837 in Northfield, Massachusetts, the sixth of nine children. His father died when Dwight was four, leaving the family in poverty. He had little formal education and moved to Boston at seventeen to work in a shoe store.

His employer's condition was that he attend Sunday school. It was there that his teacher Edward Kimball sought him out in the back room of the shop in 1855 and asked if he was willing to give his life to Christ. Moody was. It was, as he later described it, the day everything changed.

He moved to Chicago, prospered in business, and might have remained prosperous — but in 1860 he gave it up for full-time ministry among the poor of the city. He built a Sunday school in the slums that grew into what became Moody Church. He partnered with singer Ira Sankey, and together they conducted evangelistic campaigns across Britain and North America through the 1870s and 1880s that drew hundreds of thousands.

He founded Northfield schools in Massachusetts, and in 1886 established the school that became Moody Bible Institute — training ordinary men and women for ministry without requiring the formal education he himself had never received.

He died in 1899. It is estimated he preached to more than one hundred million people — each one brought face to face with a God who, as Moody put it, is not angry but loves them.

---

## Ed McCully (1927–1956)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=ed-mccully
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ed_McCully
Nationality: American · Tradition: Plymouth Brethren · Region: Latin America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: missions, sacrifice, martyrdom, faithfulness, obedience

Ed McCully was born in 1927 in Iowa. At Wheaton College he became close friends with Jim Elliot, bound by a shared restlessness for missions. McCully went on to law school at Marquette University — but after reading the Old Testament book of Nehemiah and corresponding with Elliot about the claims of Christ on a life, he withdrew the day before his second year was to begin, choosing missionary training instead of a legal career.

He married Marilou Hobolth in 1951, and the couple sailed for Ecuador the following year, serving among the Quichua people at the Arajuno mission station. There McCully joined Elliot, Nate Saint, Roger Youderian, and Peter Fleming in the slow, careful work of trying to reach the Huaorani — a tribe that had killed every outsider who had come near them.

On 8 January 1956, the five men were speared to death on a Curaray River sandbar. McCully's body was recovered downriver by local Quichua Indians but was swept away again before it could be brought back for burial; he alone of the five has no grave.

McCully had given up a promising career for something the world could not see the value of — trusting, as Scripture teaches, that a life is not saved by what it accumulates, but is redeemed only through faith in Christ, who gave His own life first.

---

## Elisabeth Elliot (1926–2015)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=elisabeth-elliot
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Elisabeth_Elliot
Nationality: American · Tradition: Plymouth Brethren / evangelical · Region: Latin America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: missions, martyrdom, forgiveness, suffering, faithfulness

Elisabeth Elliot was born in Brussels in 1926 to American missionary parents. She studied Greek at Wheaton College, where she met Jim Elliot. On 8 January 1956, Jim and four fellow missionaries were killed by the Waodani people of Ecuador — a tribe that had killed every outsider who approached them. Elisabeth was twenty-nine, with a ten-month-old daughter, Valerie.

She did not go home. In 1958, with Rachel Saint — sister of pilot Nate Saint, also killed — Elisabeth and Valerie went to live among the Waodani. For two years she lived in their community, learning their language. The tribe that had killed five missionaries came to faith in Christ, including several of the men who had carried out the killings.

She left Ecuador in 1963 and spent the rest of her long life writing and speaking. Through Gates of Splendour told the story of the five missionaries. Shadow of the Almighty preserved Jim's letters and journals. A Path Through Suffering drew on all she had lived.

She died in 2015. Her most quoted words: "God is God. Because he is God, he is worthy of my trust and obedience. I will find rest nowhere but in his will."

---

## Eric Liddell (1902–1945)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=eric-liddell
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eric_Liddell
Nationality: Scottish · Tradition: Congregationalist evangelical · Region: Asia · Era: 20th Century
Topics: faithfulness, sacrifice, missions, perseverance, suffering

Eric Liddell was born in Tianjin, China in 1902, the son of Scottish missionaries. He grew up in Scotland, excelled at rugby and athletics, and by 1924 was Britain's finest sprinter, widely expected to win gold in the 100 metres at the Paris Olympics.

His heat was scheduled for a Sunday. Liddell withdrew. He held the conviction that Sunday was set apart for worship. He switched to the 400 metres — not his event — trained intensively, and won gold, setting a world record.

He returned to China in 1925 as a missionary. When the Japanese invaded in 1937 he sent his wife Florence and their daughters to safety in Canada but remained himself. In 1943 he was interned at the Weifang camp in Shandong, where he organised sport for children, taught science, and continued to preach and counsel. He gave up his place on a prisoner exchange to a pregnant woman. In February 1945 he died of a brain tumour in the camp, aged forty-three — six months before liberation. Fellow internee Langdon Gilkey later described him as the most remarkable person he had ever met.

His life asks a persistent question: what does faithfulness actually cost? Liddell answered it at Paris in 1924 — and answered it again, more quietly, in everything that followed.

---

## F. F. Bruce (1910–1990)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=ff-bruce
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/F._F._Bruce
Nationality: Scottish · Tradition: Plymouth Brethren · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 20th Century
Topics: Scripture, biblical scholarship, faithfulness, gospel

Frederick Fyvie Bruce was born in 1910 in Elgin, Scotland, the son of an itinerant Plymouth Brethren evangelist. Trained in classics at Aberdeen, Cambridge, and Vienna, he might easily have spent his career in secular scholarship — instead he devoted it to demonstrating that the New Testament could withstand the fullest scrutiny of critical history.

Bruce taught Greek at Edinburgh and Leeds before becoming, in 1947, the first head of Biblical History and Literature at Sheffield, and later, from 1959 to 1978, Rylands Professor of Biblical Criticism at Manchester. His 1943 book Are the New Testament Documents Reliable? became one of the most influential evangelical works of the century, arguing that rigorous historical inquiry, honestly pursued, only strengthens confidence in the Gospels rather than undermining them. He wrote nearly fifty books and supervised around fifty doctoral students who went on to shape evangelical biblical scholarship worldwide, while remaining, his whole life, a simple member of his Brethren assembly rather than an ordained clergyman — in keeping with the Brethren conviction that no Christian needs a special office to serve Christ or handle his word.

For Bruce, that conviction was not incidental to his scholarship but its foundation: Scripture's authority rests on what God has done in Christ and preserved in his word, not on the credentials of those who study it.

---

## Fanny Crosby (1820–1915)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=fanny-crosby
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fanny_Crosby
Nationality: American · Tradition: Methodist evangelical · Region: North America · Era: 19th Century
Topics: praise, worship, perseverance, grace, prayer
Hymns: Blessed Assurance, To God Be the Glory, Safe in the Arms of Jesus, Near the Cross, Rescue the Perishing, All the Way My Savior Leads Me, Draw Me Nearer, Give Me Jesus, He Hideth My Soul, My Savior First of All, Praise Him! Praise Him!, Redeemed, Tell Me the Story of Jesus

Fanny Crosby was born in New York in 1820. At six weeks of age, a mistaken medical treatment permanently destroyed her sight. She would be blind for ninety-four years.

She came to evangelical faith in 1850 and from that point wrote an estimated 8,000–9,000 hymn texts, often composing several per day by dictating from memory. Her best-known hymns — Blessed Assurance, To God Be the Glory, Safe in the Arms of Jesus — share a single theological weight: the assurance of salvation in Christ, the certainty of God's love for the sinner.

When she wrote "This is my story, this is my song, praising my Saviour all the day long," she was speaking her own settled conviction.

She said of her blindness: "If perfect earthly sight were offered me tomorrow I would not accept it. I might not have sung hymns to the praise of God if I had been distracted by the beautiful things about me."

---

## Felix Manz (c. 1498–1527)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=felix-manz
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Felix_Manz
Nationality: Swiss · Tradition: Anabaptist (Swiss Brethren) · Region: Europe · Era: Reformation
Topics: martyrdom, conscience, courage, believer's baptism

Felix Manz was born around 1498 in Zurich, the son of a canon at the Grossmünster, and was fluent in Hebrew, Greek, and Latin by early adulthood. He began as a follower of the reformer Huldrych Zwingli, but he and fellow student Conrad Grebel grew convinced that Zwingli's reforms did not go far enough — particularly on infant baptism, which they could not find commanded in Scripture.

On 21 January 1525, in defiance of the Zurich council, Manz and Grebel took part in the first adult baptisms of the Reformation era, effectively founding what became known as the Swiss Brethren. Manz spent the following two years as an evangelist and translator for the growing movement, preaching that baptism belonged only to those who could personally confess faith in Christ — a conviction he refused to abandon even as the authorities who had once been his teachers turned against him.

The Zurich council, unwilling to tolerate a rival form of the church, made adult rebaptism a capital offense. Manz was arrested and, on 5 January 1527, drowned in the Limmat River — a punishment chosen, grimly, to mock the very rite he had died defending. He was the first Protestant executed by fellow Protestants for his faith. His reported last words, “Into thy hands, O God, I commend my spirit,” echoed Christ's own words from the cross: a young man's conscience, staked entirely on the mercy of the God he was trusting to receive him.

---

## Frances Ridley Havergal (1836–1879)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=frances-ridley-havergal
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frances_Ridley_Havergal
Nationality: English · Tradition: Anglican evangelical · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 19th Century
Topics: surrender, faithfulness, grace, worship, consecration
Hymns: Like a River Glorious, Take My Life, and Let It Be, Who Is on the Lord's Side?

Frances Ridley Havergal (1836–1879) was born in Astley, Worcestershire, the youngest child of William Henry Havergal — a clergyman, composer, and hymn writer. From childhood she was drawn to Scripture and language: she had memorised the New Testament, the Psalms, and much of the prophets before adulthood, and she read Greek and Hebrew.

She came to evangelical faith around 1851 — what she described as a deliberate, decisive trust: "I did trust the Lord Jesus." In 1873, reading a booklet called All For Jesus deepened this into what she called full consecration, which became the wellspring of her most distinctive hymns: Take My Life and Let It Be, Like a River Glorious, and Who Is on the Lord's Side? Take My Life moves verse by verse through every faculty and possession — voice, hands, feet, intellect, will, heart, love, money — and does not leave the singer anywhere safe.

Havergal died of peritonitis at forty-two in June 1879. Her final recorded words: "If I am going, it is too good to be true. Come, Lord Jesus, come quickly! Splendid to be so near the gates of heaven. So beautiful to go." She had spent her short life writing of consecration and trust — and she died as she had written. Her hymns remain because they give congregations words for a surrender they know they have not yet fully made.

---

## Francis Schaeffer (1912–1984)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=francis-schaeffer
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Francis_Schaeffer
Nationality: American · Tradition: Reformed Presbyterian (RPCES) · Region: Europe · Era: 20th Century
Topics: grace, faith, Scripture, gospel, service

Francis Schaeffer was born in 1912 near Philadelphia into a home with little religious interest. As a teenager he began testing the great questions of philosophy against the Bible, reading it cover to cover over several months, and around 1930 he came to trust in Christ. He trained at Westminster and Faith Theological Seminary under scholars committed to the full trustworthiness of Scripture, and in 1938 became the first minister ordained in the newly formed Bible Presbyterian Church.

In 1948 Schaeffer moved with his wife Edith to Switzerland. A few years later, troubled that his own faith had grown more about correct doctrine than a living love for Christ and others, he went through a season of painful self-examination that reshaped his ministry. Out of it came L’Abri (‘The Shelter’), which he and Edith opened in their home in Huémoz in 1955 — a place where the honest questions of doubting students and travelers were met with patient answers and genuine welcome, not dismissal. Through books such as The God Who Is There and How Should We Then Live?, he helped a generation of evangelicals engage seriously with modern culture without abandoning confidence in the gospel.

Schaeffer’s central conviction, tested in his own season of doubt before it appeared in any book, was that Christ alone answers what philosophy cannot: not by argument winning the point, but by grace meeting a person exactly where their questions have left them stranded.

---

## George Beverly Shea (1909–2013)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=george-beverly-shea
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/George_Beverly_Shea
Nationality: Canadian-American · Tradition: Wesleyan Methodist · Region: North America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: evangelism, faithfulness, grace, gospel
Hymns: I'd Rather Have Jesus, The Wonder of It All

George Beverly Shea was born in 1909 in Winchester, Ontario, the son of a Wesleyan Methodist minister. He worked as an insurance clerk in New York while singing on NBC radio, until a friend showed him a poem, “I'd Rather Have Jesus,” and urged him to set it to music. The tune came to him in minutes at the piano — and became the song most associated with his name.

In the early 1940s Shea met a young evangelist named Billy Graham on a Chicago radio program; in November 1947 he became the first staff member of the Billy Graham Evangelistic Association, a partnership that lasted until his death. For more than sixty years Shea sang before Graham's crusades on every continent, reportedly performing live to more people than anyone in history, always the same deep, unadorned baritone offering the same simple invitation to trust Christ.

When Graham first invited him to join the ministry, Shea asked whether he would be expected to talk between songs the way other gospel singers did. Graham chuckled: “I hope not.” Shea's calling was never to draw attention to his own voice but to let it fade behind the message it carried. He kept singing until he was over one hundred years old, content, as his signature song put it, to have Jesus rather than houses, or lands, or fame — and to have nothing else besides.

---

## George Matheson (1842–1906)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=george-matheson
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/George_Matheson
Nationality: Scottish · Tradition: Church of Scotland · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 19th Century
Topics: suffering, hymns, grace, perseverance, blindness
Hymns: O Love That Wilt Not Let Me Go

George Matheson was born in Glasgow in 1842 and was already losing his sight as a boy; by the time he finished his studies at the University of Glasgow, where he graduated first in his class, he was completely blind. His fiancée, learning the blindness was permanent, ended their engagement rather than share that future with him — a loss Matheson carried the rest of his life, aided afterward mainly by his devoted sister, who learned Hebrew and Greek to help him continue studying for the ministry.

He was licensed to preach in 1866 and served the parish of Innellan from 1868, then St Bernard's, Edinburgh, from 1886. He became a compelling preacher despite — or because of — never seeing his congregation's faces. In 1882, on an evening he later described only as one of “the most severe mental suffering,” he wrote the hymn “O Love That Wilt Not Let Me Go” in about five minutes, without revision. He never publicly explained what suffering prompted it.

The hymn's central image — a love that will not let go, even of someone with nothing left to offer in return — is Matheson's theology in miniature: he had lost his sight and the marriage he had planned, and had nothing to bring God but his need. “O Cross that liftest up my head, I dare not ask to fly from thee,” he wrote in the same hymn. He died in 1906.

---

## George Müller (1805–1898)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=george-muller
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/George_M%C3%BCller
Nationality: German / British · Tradition: Plymouth Brethren · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 19th Century
Topics: prayer, faithfulness, grace, missions

George Müller was born in Kroppenstedt, Prussia in 1805. By his own account he was a habitual liar and petty thief from childhood. His conversion came in 1825 at a small prayer meeting in Halle, where he felt the weight of his sin and the reality of Christ's forgiveness.

He came to England, settled in Bristol in 1832, and in 1836 founded his first home for orphaned children — as a deliberate experiment: to demonstrate through the running of the orphanage that God hears and answers prayer. He would never publicly appeal for funds. He would tell God what was needed, and trust God to provide.

Over sixty years, Müller cared for more than 10,000 orphaned children at the Ashley Down complex. He kept meticulous, dated records of answered prayer which he published so others could read the evidence. He died at ninety-two in 1898.

---

## George Verwer (1938–2023)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=george-verwer
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/George_Verwer
Nationality: American · Tradition: Interdenominational evangelical · Region: North America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: missions, evangelism, grace, perseverance, gospel

George Verwer was born in New Jersey in 1938 and converted to faith in Christ at sixteen at a Billy Graham meeting in Madison Square Garden. The following year he began distributing Gospel literature in Mexico with a handful of friends; by 1962 he had formally founded Operation Mobilisation, which would grow to thousands of workers in more than a hundred countries.

Verwer’s two great convictions shaped everything OM became. The first was that the gospel was owed to the whole world, not a comfortable few. The second was what he called “messiology” — his term for the biblical truth that God works through fallible, stumbling people, not around them. He launched ship ministries to take the gospel to ports inaccessible to conventional mission teams: the MV Logos in 1970, the Doulos in 1977. He called himself “God’s Bungler” — not in false modesty but as theology.

He handed OM’s leadership over in 2003 but continued preaching, writing, and encouraging missionaries until shortly before his death in London on 14 April 2023, aged eighty-four. His legacy is hundreds of thousands of lives reached and a generation of workers from every continent who took seriously the same passage that had once gripped a sixteen-year-old in Madison Square Garden: Christ died for sins, once for all, the righteous for the unrighteous, to bring us to God.

---

## George Whitefield (1714–1770)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=george-whitefield
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/George_Whitefield
Nationality: English · Tradition: Anglican evangelical (Calvinist Methodist) · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 18th Century
Topics: conversion, gospel, grace, missions
Note: Whitefield owned enslaved people who worked at his Bethesda orphanage in Georgia, and he actively campaigned for the legalization of slavery in the colony, which had originally banned it. This was a grave moral failure at odds with the gospel of grace he preached to every race and class, and is acknowledged here plainly alongside his historical significance to the Great Awakening.

George Whitefield was born in Gloucester in 1714. At Oxford he fell in with the Wesley brothers, and after prolonged spiritual struggle received evangelical conversion grounded in the assurance of salvation by grace through faith alone.

What followed was a preaching ministry without contemporary equal. Whitefield had extraordinary vocal power. When indoor venues were closed to him, he preached outdoors — tens of thousands at a time, on both sides of the Atlantic. Benjamin Franklin attended one Philadelphia meeting determined not to give to the collection and found by the end he had emptied his pockets.

He made thirteen Atlantic voyages and was a central figure in the First Great Awakening, working alongside Jonathan Edwards. He died in Newburyport, Massachusetts in 1770, still preaching.

Note: Whitefield owned enslaved people at his Georgia orphanage — a serious moral failing that casts a shadow on his legacy.

---

## George Williams (1821–1905)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=george-williams
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/George_Williams_(philanthropist)
Nationality: English · Tradition: Congregationalist evangelical · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 19th Century
Topics: discipleship, service, faithfulness, gospel, compassion

George Williams was born in 1821 in Dulverton, Somerset, England, the youngest of seven sons in a farming family. Converted from Anglicanism to a more evangelical Congregationalist faith as a teenager, he moved to London in 1841 to work as a draper's apprentice — a trade that kept young men working long hours and living together above city shops, often far from any church or family influence.

Concerned for the spiritual welfare of his fellow drapery workers, Williams gathered eleven of them above a shop in St Paul's Churchyard on 6 June 1844 to pray and study Scripture together. That small meeting became the Young Men's Christian Association — the YMCA — an organization built on the conviction that young men adrift in an indifferent city needed, above all else, to know Christ.

Williams went on to become sole owner of his drapery firm and was knighted by Queen Victoria in 1894 for a lifetime of Christian philanthropy, including work with the London City Mission and the Railway Mission.
