Yet the YMCA always remained his central legacy — a movement that spread worldwide, all traceable back to a handful of young men praying above a shop.

Williams died in 1905, having spent sixty years insisting that the answer to a young man's spiritual danger was never self-improvement, but the grace of Christ, offered freely and received by faith.

---

## Gil Seon-ju 길선주 (1869–1935)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=gil-seon-ju
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gil_Seon-ju
Nationality: Korean · Tradition: Presbyterian · Region: Asia · Era: 20th Century
Topics: revival, repentance, prayer, perseverance
Note: Before his conversion, Gil spent roughly a decade as a devotee of Korean folk mysticism, and some historians trace continuity between those earlier ascetic disciplines — extended fasting, all-night prayer — and his later, distinctively fervent prayer style, including his founding of Korea's dawn prayer meeting. This does not cast doubt on his conversion or his Christian ministry, which are well documented and orthodox, but is noted here for the reader's discernment.

Gil Seon-ju was born in 1869 in Korea's South Pyeongan Province. For about ten years he pursued Korean folk mysticism, seeking enlightenment through fasting and ascetic discipline, before a friend introduced him to Christian teaching in 1897. He was converted, and became one of the first seven Koreans ordained as Presbyterian ministers, in 1907, pastoring Jangdaehyeon Church in Pyongyang.

That same year, at an evening meeting, Gil stood and confessed publicly to a specific sin — money he had wrongly kept while settling a deceased friend's estate — saying it stood between the Korean church and God's blessing. His confession triggered a wave of repentance across the congregation that became known as the 1907 Pyongyang Great Revival, a turning point for Korean Protestant Christianity. In 1919 Gil signed Korea's Declaration of Independence as one of thirty-three national representatives and was imprisoned by Japanese authorities for over two years.

Gil's revival began the moment he stopped defending himself. He did not call the congregation to try harder; he showed them, in his own confession, that the only way forward was to stop hiding sin and receive the forgiveness Christ freely offers — a lesson every person in that room, and every reader since, still needs.

---

## Gladys Aylward (1902–1970)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=gladys-aylward
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gladys_Aylward
Nationality: British · Tradition: Independent evangelical · Region: Asia · Era: 20th Century
Topics: missions, faith, courage, service, trust, perseverance

Gladys Aylward was born in Edmonton, London in 1902 and worked as a parlour maid. She felt called to China but was rejected by the China Inland Mission training college on the grounds that her academic abilities were insufficient. She did not accept the rejection as final.

Saving every penny of her wages, she purchased a train ticket via the Trans-Siberian Railway and arrived in Yangcheng, Shanxi Province in 1930. She joined veteran missionary Jeannie Lawson, running an inn for muleteers — standing in the street to pull mule trains in by the bridle and, once the men were eating, telling them stories from the Bible. She eventually became a Chinese citizen, commissioned by the local Mandarin as a foot inspector, and built deep roots in the community.

When Japanese forces invaded and bombed Yangcheng in 1940, Gladys led one hundred orphan children on a two-week journey over mountain passes to safety in Sian (Xi’an). She was seriously ill throughout the crossing, but got every child there.

She later settled permanently in Taiwan, caring for orphaned children until her death in January 1970.

Rejected by the missions board, she went anyway. Dismissed as too ordinary, God used her extraordinarily. Her life is a testimony that calling comes from God — and God does not consult admissions panels.

---

## H.A. Ironside (1876–1951)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=ha-ironside
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harry_A._Ironside
Nationality: Canadian-American · Tradition: Plymouth Brethren / Nondenominational evangelical · Region: North America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: gospel, Scripture, grace, preaching, prophecy, dispensationalism, biblical scholarship

Henry Allan Ironside was born in Toronto in 1876 and raised by his widowed mother after his father died of typhoid when Harry was just two. The family moved to Los Angeles in 1886, and at eleven years old Harry gathered neighbourhood children under a canvas tent and taught them the Bible himself, averaging sixty each session. In 1888 he heard Dwight L. Moody preach to eight thousand people — and resolved to do the same. After months wrestling with whether he was truly born again, he trusted Christ at thirteen. 'I rested on the Word of God and confessed Christ as my Saviour,' he said.

By sixteen he was a full-time Salvation Army preacher delivering four hundred sermons a year. He later joined the Plymouth Brethren, moving in the same world as George Müller, and from 1916 preached nearly seven thousand sermons to more than a million and a quarter listeners.

In 1929 he was called to pastor Moody Church in Chicago — the church built in the tradition of Dwight L. Moody — where he served for nineteen years, filling its four-thousand-seat auditorium Sunday after Sunday. He served as president of the Africa Inland Mission from 1942.

Ironside published more than a hundred books, commentaries on virtually every book of the Bible, written with the clarity of a man who had left school after the eighth grade but never stopped learning from Scripture. He died in New Zealand in January 1951, still mid-tour, still bearing witness.

---

## Harriet Tubman (c. 1822–1913)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=harriet-tubman
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harriet_Tubman
Nationality: American · Tradition: African Methodist Episcopal Zion · Region: North America · Era: 19th Century
Topics: freedom, providence, prayer, courage, suffering, faith
Note: Tubman interpreted the vivid visions and premonitions that followed a childhood head injury as direct revelations from God, and her contemporaries and biographers record these experiences as central to her sense of calling. This kind of ongoing personal revelation is not universally accepted across evangelical traditions, though her lifelong trust in Christ and reliance on prayer are not in question.

Harriet Tubman was born Araminta Ross into slavery around 1822 in Dorchester County, Maryland. As a teenager, a blow from an overseer left her with lifelong seizures and, she believed, visions from God. Raised in her enslaved parents' Methodist faith, she escaped alone in 1849, walking ninety miles to freedom in Pennsylvania.

She became known as "Moses." Over the next decade she returned south thirteen times, leading about seventy enslaved people — including her parents — north along the Underground Railroad, and never lost one. She later said, "God set the North Star in the heavens; he gave me the strength in my limbs; he meant I should be free." In the Civil War she served as a Union scout and nurse, and in June 1863 guided an armed raid freeing more than seven hundred enslaved people on the Combahee River.

Tubman credited none of it to her courage. Thomas Garrett, the Quaker abolitionist who worked alongside her, said he had "never met with any person of any color who had more confidence in the voice of God, as spoken direct to her soul." The freedom she risked her life for pointed to a freedom already hers as a gift — salvation held out not to the deserving but to anyone who trusts Christ. In old age she opened her Auburn, New York home to the elderly poor and worshipped at Thompson AME Zion Church, where she was buried in 1913 beneath the words "Servant of God, Well Done."

---

## Helen Roseveare (1925–2016)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=helen-roseveare
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Helen_Roseveare
Nationality: English · Tradition: WEC International (interdenominational evangelical) · Region: Africa · Era: 20th Century
Topics: missions, suffering, perseverance, grace

Helen Roseveare was born in England in 1925 and came to faith in Christ in 1945 while a medical student at Cambridge, through the university’s Christian Union. Trained as a doctor, she sailed for the Belgian Congo in 1953 with the mission agency WEC International, determined to combine medicine with the gospel.

At Ibambi, and later at a former leprosy camp called Nebobongo, she built a hospital and training school from nothing, training generations of Congolese paramedics. In 1964, during the violent Simba Rebellion, Roseveare was taken prisoner by rebel soldiers and held for months, suffering brutal assault. She later said her survival owed much to villagers she had previously cared for, who intervened on her behalf. Remarkably, she returned to Congo in 1966 to found another medical centre at Nyankunde, serving until 1973, and then spent decades travelling and writing about what she had learned of God in the midst of suffering.

One story she told often: a Congolese girl named Ruth prayed simply and specifically for a hot water bottle and a doll, to comfort a dying baby and its grieving young sister — and both arrived, unexpectedly, in a parcel that same day. Roseveare’s whole life testified to that same childlike confidence: that grace, not our strength or worthiness, is what God delights to give those who simply ask.

---

## Henry Martyn (1781–1812)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=henry-martyn
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Henry_Martyn
Nationality: British · Tradition: Anglican · Region: Asia · Era: 19th Century
Topics: missions, sacrifice, Scripture, prayer

Henry Martyn was born in Truro, Cornwall, in 1781. He was a brilliant scholar — Senior Wrangler at Cambridge in 1801, the highest mathematical distinction of his generation — whose ambitions for academic fame were overturned by conversion and the example of William Carey.

Ordained and appointed as a chaplain of the East India Company, he sailed for India in 1805. He learned Urdu, Persian, and Arabic and threw himself into Bible translation. He completed a New Testament in Urdu (1809), supervised Persian and Arabic translations, and travelled to Persia for the sake of a revised Persian text — debating Islamic scholars in Shiraz on the claims of Christ.

His journals record a relentless conflict between spiritual longing and physical collapse. He suffered from tuberculosis throughout his missionary years and died in Tocat, Turkey, in 1812, aged 31, while attempting to return home.

"I cannot find it in my heart to grieve," he wrote, "for what Christ thinks worth dying for." He had given the New Testament to three language groups. His life was brief; his fruit enduring.

---

## Horatio Spafford (1828–1888)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=horatio-spafford
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Horatio_Spafford
Nationality: American · Tradition: Presbyterian evangelical · Region: North America · Era: 19th Century
Topics: suffering, perseverance, grace, faithfulness, gospel
Hymns: It Is Well with My Soul

Horatio Spafford was born in Troy, New York in 1828. A Chicago lawyer and businessman, he was a close friend and financial backer of the evangelist D.L. Moody and a man of active evangelical faith.

In 1871 the Great Chicago Fire consumed most of his real estate investments. In November 1873 he sent his wife Anna and their four daughters — Annie, Maggie, Bessie, and Tanetta — to France on the ocean liner Ville du Havre. He planned to follow shortly. The ship was struck by an iron sailing vessel and sank in twelve minutes. All four girls drowned. Anna survived and cabled him two words: "Saved alone."

Spafford sailed immediately to meet his wife. When the ship passed over the place where his daughters had drowned, he wrote the words of a hymn. Philip Bliss — who is also on this site — set them to music: "When peace like a river attendeth my way, / When sorrows like sea billows roll; / Whatever my lot, thou hast taught me to say, / It is well, it is well with my soul."

The hymn does not deny the grief. It claims something the grief cannot reach. Spafford later lost a son to scarlet fever. He died in Jerusalem in 1888, aged fifty-nine, having served the poor there for seven years.

---

## Horatius Bonar (1808–1889)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=horatius-bonar
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Horatius_Bonar
Nationality: Scottish · Tradition: Free Church of Scotland · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 19th Century
Topics: grace, worship, praise, gospel, prayer
Hymns: I Heard the Voice of Jesus Say, I Was a Wandering Sheep, A Mind at Perfect Peace, Blessed Be God, Our God, Done Is the Work That Saves, Here, O Our Lord, I Hear the Words of Love, No Blood, No Altar Now

Horatius Bonar was born in Edinburgh in 1808 into a long tradition of Presbyterian ministry. Ordained in 1838, he served in Kelso. When the Disruption of 1843 split the Church of Scotland, he walked out with those who formed the Free Church, unwilling to accept state interference in the appointment of ministers.

He became one of the most prolific Scottish hymn writers of the Victorian era, producing over 600 hymns notable for their warmth and their consistent focus on the person of Christ.

I Heard the Voice of Jesus Say is structured around three invitations — to rest, to drink, to come into the light — each answered with a personal response of faith. It is the gospel in dialogue: Jesus invites, the soul comes, and finds exactly what He promised. Bonar's hymns are windows onto the mercy of Jesus — they point through themselves toward Him.

---

## Hsi Shengmo 席勝魔 (c. 1836–1896)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=hsi-shengmo
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Xi_Shengmo
Nationality: Chinese · Tradition: China Inland Mission (interdenominational evangelical) · Region: Asia · Era: 19th Century
Topics: conversion, missions, perseverance, grace

Hsi Shengmo was born around 1836 near Linfen, in China's Shanxi province, and rose to be a respected Confucian scholar. After the death of his first wife left him without hope, he turned to opium and sank for years into addiction, despised by the community that had once honored him.

Introduced to Christian teaching through an English Methodist missionary, he read the Gospel account of the crucifixion and wept, overwhelmed by the discovery that Christ had died for a man as broken as he was. Freed from opium through prayer alone, he took the name Shengmo — 'conqueror of demons' — and was baptized in 1880. Hudson Taylor ordained him in 1886 as pastor over a wide district, where he went on to found some forty opium refuges across four provinces, each one doubling as a small church.

Hsi never treated his own recovery as proof of willpower. He told his story the way Scripture tells every conversion story: not a man reforming himself, but a sinner laid hold of by grace, and made new by faith in the One he had wept over — Christ crucified for him.

---

## Hudson Taylor (1832–1905)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=hudson-taylor
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/James_Hudson_Taylor
Nationality: English · Tradition: Nondenominational evangelical · Region: Asia · Era: 19th Century
Topics: missions, faithfulness, prayer, perseverance

James Hudson Taylor was born in Barnsley, Yorkshire in 1832. He came to faith at seventeen, reading a gospel tract alone at home, and felt immediately drawn toward China. He arrived in Shanghai in 1854 and worked under existing mission societies, but grew convinced they were not reaching inland China.

In 1865, during a personal crisis on a beach in Brighton, he committed to founding the China Inland Mission. Its principles were distinctive: recruit from any Protestant denomination; make no public appeals for funds; adopt Chinese dress and customs. The CIM became the largest Protestant missionary organisation in China. The cost was enormous — Taylor lost his first wife and several children in China, and suffered recurring illness.

He died in Changsha, Hunan in 1905. His conviction throughout: God's commands are sufficient reason to obey them regardless of the cost.

---

## Hugh Latimer (c. 1487–1555)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=hugh-latimer
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hugh_Latimer
Nationality: English · Tradition: Anglican Reformer · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: Reformation
Topics: martyrdom, preaching, grace, courage

Hugh Latimer was born around 1487 in Thurcaston, Leicestershire, and became a fellow of Clare College, Cambridge. He began as a defender of the old Catholic faith, but around 1524 study of Scripture — reportedly through conversation with fellow Cambridge scholar Thomas Bilney — turned him toward the Reformation. He became a celebrated preacher, serving as Bishop of Worcester and later as chaplain to the young King Edward VI, known for plain, vivid sermons that applied Scripture bluntly to the abuses of his day, regardless of who was listening.

When the Catholic Mary I took the throne, Latimer's outspoken preaching made him an early target. He was imprisoned and tried for heresy alongside fellow bishops Thomas Cranmer and Nicholas Ridley, and on 16 October 1555 he was burned at the stake with Ridley outside Balliol College, Oxford.

As the fire was lit, Latimer turned to his fellow martyr and spoke words that have outlasted both of them: “Play the man, Master Ridley; we shall this day light such a candle, by God's grace, in England, as I trust shall never be put out.” He was right — Latimer died an old man in the flames, but the message he had preached for decades, that sinners are saved by Christ's grace and not by rite or ritual, kept spreading in England long after the smoke cleared.

---

## Huldrych Zwingli (1484–1531)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=huldrych-zwingli
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Huldrych_Zwingli
Nationality: Swiss · Tradition: Reformed · Region: Europe · Era: Reformation
Topics: Reformation, Scripture, grace, preaching

Huldrych Zwingli was born in 1484 in Wildhaus, Switzerland, and ordained a Catholic priest in 1506. Appointed people's priest at the Grossmünster in Zurich in 1519, he broke with the conventional practice of preaching from the lectionary and instead preached verse by verse through entire books of Scripture — a method that let the Bible itself, rather than church tradition, set the agenda for what Zurich heard on Sunday.

Independently of Martin Luther, Zwingli arrived at many of the same convictions: salvation by grace through faith, Scripture as the final authority, and a rejection of practices he judged unscriptural, including the veneration of images and the doctrine that the Mass re-offered Christ's sacrifice. He argued instead that the Lord's Supper was a memorial of a sacrifice completed once for all — a disagreement with Luther over the nature of Christ's presence in the meal that split the two reformers permanently and, with them, the wider Reformation.

In 1531, war broke out between Zurich's Protestant cantons and their Catholic neighbors. Zwingli accompanied Zurich's forces as chaplain and was killed at the Battle of Kappel, one of about five hundred dead in an hour of fighting. He had written years earlier, after surviving the plague, of submitting entirely to God's purpose — words that outlasted him, along with a conviction that had already reshaped Swiss Christianity: that the church's authority is Scripture alone, and its foundation is Christ's finished work, not man's.

---

## Ira Sankey (1840–1908)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=ira-sankey
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ira_Sankey
Nationality: American · Tradition: Methodist evangelical · Region: North America · Era: 19th Century
Topics: worship, praise, grace, hymns, gospel
Hymns: The Ninety and Nine, Beneath the Cross of Jesus, A Shelter in the Time of Storm, Trusting Jesus, Faith is the Victory, Who Is on the Lord's Side?, I Am Praying for You, Under His Wings

Ira Sankey was born in 1840 in Edinburg, Pennsylvania, and came to faith in his late teens at a revival meeting. Years later, as a young YMCA delegate attending a convention in Indianapolis in 1870, Sankey was asked to lead a hymn — and a preacher named Dwight L. Moody, listening nearby, told him afterward, 'I have been looking for you for the last eight years.' After six months of hesitation, Sankey gave up his career to become Moody's song leader, a partnership that would last until Moody's death in 1899.

Sankey's rich voice and simple reed organ accompaniment carried Moody's preaching across Britain and America through the 1870s and 1880s, reaching enormous crowds. He compiled and expanded Sacred Songs and Solos, first published in 1873, which grew to include over a thousand hymns, and composed tunes for gospel songs including 'The Ninety and Nine' and 'A Shelter in the Time of Storm.'

By 1903 Sankey had gone blind from glaucoma, yet he continued serving the church until his death in 1908. His hymns rarely dwelt on his own gifts or losses; again and again they pointed hearers back to a Saviour who seeks and saves those who cannot save themselves — grace offered, as Sankey sang it, to be simply received by faith, not earned by effort.

---

## Isaac Watts (1674–1748)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=isaac-watts
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isaac_Watts
Nationality: English · Tradition: Nonconformist / Independent · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 18th Century
Topics: praise, worship, grace, gospel
Hymns: When I Survey the Wondrous Cross, Joy to the World, Jesus Shall Reign, Alas! and Did My Savior Bleed, At the Cross, I Sing the Mighty Power of God, Not All the Blood of Beasts, O God, Our Help in Ages Past

Isaac Watts was born in Southampton in 1674, the son of a Nonconformist deacon who had been imprisoned for his dissenting faith. Before Watts, English congregational singing was confined almost entirely to metrical Psalms. He argued that the church should sing its own experience of the risen Christ.

His hymns remain among the most widely sung in Christian history. When I Survey the Wondrous Cross moves from contemplation to complete surrender: "Love so amazing, so divine, demands my soul, my life, my all." O God Our Help in Ages Past sets Psalm 90 within a Christian understanding of eternity.

He wrote over 750 hymns before his death in 1748, spending his final thirty-six years largely confined by chronic illness — writing from his room, giving the church words to praise Jesus.

---

## Isobel Kuhn (1901–1957)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=isobel-kuhn
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isobel_Kuhn
Nationality: Canadian · Tradition: China Inland Mission (interdenominational evangelical) · Region: Asia · Era: 20th Century
Topics: missions, perseverance, grace, faithfulness, conversion

Isobel Miller Kuhn was born in Toronto in 1901 and raised in a Presbyterian home, but by her last year at the University of British Columbia she had drifted into agnosticism, abandoning prayer and church. In 1921, undone by a broken engagement and at the edge of despair, she prayed a desperate bargain: ‘God, if there be a God... if You will give me peace, I will give You my whole life.’ The peace came, and so did the surrender.

Three years later, hearing missionary J. O. Fraser describe the Lisu people of Yunnan, China, bound by fear of spirits, Isobel knew where that surrendered life was headed. She sailed with the China Inland Mission in 1928 and married fellow missionary John Kuhn the following year in Kunming. From 1934 to 1950 the Kuhns lived among the Lisu, where Isobel pioneered the ‘Rainy Season Bible School,’ timed to the farming calendar so Lisu believers could be trained as evangelists to their own people. When Communist forces swept through in 1950, the family fled over a mountain pass into Burma, leaving behind a growing church. From 1952 she and John continued the same work among Lisu communities in northern Thailand, until illness forced her retirement in 1954. She spent her last years writing books that still carry her testimony that grace, once truly received, cannot be kept to oneself.

---

## J. Gresham Machen (1881–1937)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=j-gresham-machen
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J._Gresham_Machen
Nationality: American · Tradition: Presbyterian (Orthodox Presbyterian Church) · Region: North America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: Scripture, gospel, courage, faithfulness, doctrine

J. Gresham Machen was born in 1881 in Baltimore, Maryland. Educated at Johns Hopkins and Princeton, he spent 1905–06 studying under leading liberal theologians in Germany — an experience that, rather than persuading him, deepened his conviction that historic Christianity and the new theological Liberalism sweeping American seminaries were not variations on the same faith, but two different religions entirely.

Machen taught New Testament at Princeton Theological Seminary from 1906. His 1923 book Christianity and Liberalism argued, plainly and carefully, that the doctrines Liberal theology set aside — the deity of Christ, his atoning death, and salvation by grace through faith rather than human effort or moral improvement — were not incidental but were Christianity itself. Remove them, he wrote, and nothing distinctly Christian remains.

When Princeton's governance was reorganized in 1929 to accommodate more liberal leadership, Machen and several colleagues left to found Westminster Theological Seminary in Philadelphia, determined to keep training ministers in the historic faith. Suspended from Presbyterian ministry in 1935 for his continued opposition to Liberalism in the denomination's mission board, Machen helped establish a new denomination the following year, later renamed the Orthodox Presbyterian Church.

He died suddenly of pneumonia in North Dakota in January 1937, on a winter preaching tour. His gravestone bears a single Greek phrase: 'Faithful unto death' — the conviction, uncompromised to the end, that Christ alone saves, and that this good news is worth defending at any cost.

---

## Jacob DeShazer (1912–2008)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=jacob-deshazer
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jacob_DeShazer
Nationality: American · Tradition: Free Methodist · Region: North America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: grace, conversion, forgiveness, suffering, missions

Jacob DeShazer was born in 1912 in West Stayton, Oregon, and joined the US Army Air Forces before America entered the Second World War. On 18 April 1942 he served as bombardier on one of the sixteen B-25s of the Doolittle Raid, bombing Nagoya in the first American air strike on Japan. Shot down over China, he was captured by Japanese forces and endured forty months in captivity, thirty-four of them in solitary confinement, beaten and starved. Three of his fellow captured airmen were executed and a fourth died of starvation; DeShazer's own death sentence was commuted to life imprisonment.

In 1944 he persuaded a guard to lend him a Bible for three weeks. He read it over and over, and in his cell he came to trust in Christ. Released in August 1945, DeShazer trained at Seattle Pacific College and Asbury Theological Seminary, then returned to Japan in December 1948 as a Free Methodist missionary — to the same nation that had held and tortured him. He served there for thirty years. His testimony pamphlet, 'I Was a Prisoner of Japan,' was read by former Pearl Harbor commander Mitsuo Fuchida, and became part of what led Fuchida to Christ; the two former enemies later preached together.

DeShazer never described his transformation as something he achieved through willpower or resolve. It was grace received in a prison cell — undeserved, unearned, and strong enough to turn hatred for his captors into thirty years spent loving their countrymen in Christ's name.

---

## James Montgomery (1771–1854)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=james-montgomery
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/James_Montgomery_(poet)
Nationality: British · Tradition: Moravian · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 19th Century
Topics: hymn writing, abolition, justice, prayer
Hymns: Angels from the Realms of Glory, Prayer Is the Soul's Sincere Desire, Hail to the Lord's Anointed

James Montgomery was born in 1771 in Irvine, Scotland, the son of Moravian missionaries who died in the West Indies within a year of each other while he was still a boy at Fulneck School. He settled in Sheffield, England, where he edited the newspaper the Sheffield Iris for over thirty years — a role that twice landed him in prison, once for printing a poem celebrating the fall of the Bastille, once for criticizing a local magistrate.

Montgomery used his platform to campaign against slavery and against the exploitation of child chimney sweeps, convictions rooted in the same faith that shaped his poetry. Over his lifetime he wrote some four hundred hymns, several of which remain in wide use: “Angels from the Realms of Glory,” “Prayer Is the Soul's Sincere Desire,” and “Hail to the Lord's Anointed.” His hymns pair lyrical craft with plain doctrine — grace freely given, prayer as dependence rather than achievement, Christ as the anointed king before whom every human throne must eventually bow.

Montgomery never married, devoting himself instead to his writing and to Sheffield's charitable and religious causes until his death in 1854. He was honored with a public funeral, and the city later raised a monument over his grave — a tribute, fittingly, to a man whose own hymns insisted that lasting honor belongs to Christ, not to the poet who sings of him.

---

## James Strong (1822–1894)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=james-strong
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/James_Strong_(biblical_scholar)
Nationality: American · Tradition: Methodist · Region: North America · Era: 19th Century
Topics: Scripture, biblical scholarship

James Strong was born in New York City in 1822. After studies in classics and theology he served for many years as professor of exegetical theology at Drew Theological Seminary in New Jersey, and as a Methodist churchman devoted to the careful study of Scripture.

In 1890, after more than thirty years of labour, he published his Exhaustive Concordance of the Bible. Every word in the King James Bible is indexed, and each entry is linked by number to its original Hebrew or Greek term in a companion lexicon. The work was compiled by hand, before computers — a feat of sustained scholarly patience by Strong and his team that took decades to complete.

Strong's numbers, as they came to be known, became a standard reference tool that remains in daily use more than 130 years later. A preacher with no knowledge of biblical languages can look up any English word, find its number, and trace it back to its root in the original text — a doorway Strong spent his life building and leaving open.

He died in 1894. The Concordance outlasted him and is still published today, probably the most widely used Bible study reference in the English-speaking world.

---

## Jeremiah Lanphier (1809–1898)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=jeremiah-lanphier
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jeremiah_Lanphier
Nationality: American · Tradition: Dutch Reformed · Region: North America · Era: 19th Century
Topics: prayer, revival, faithfulness, gospel, conversion

Jeremiah Lanphier was born in 1809 in Coxsackie, New York. After a business as a cloth merchant failed, he became an active, faithful member of several Reformed and Presbyterian congregations in New York City, and in 1857 was hired as a lay missionary for the North Dutch Church in Lower Manhattan — a role with no formal theological training required, only a willingness to serve.

On 23 September 1857, Lanphier held the first of what he intended as a weekly noon prayer meeting for businessmen, distributing handbills inviting passers-by to 'stop 5, 10, or 20 minutes, or the whole time, as your time admits.' Only six people came, arriving late. Within weeks, as a severe financial panic gripped the city, attendance climbed into the dozens, then hundreds; by March 1858 an estimated ten thousand people were gathering daily at linked prayer meetings across Lower Manhattan, and the movement had spread to other cities across America.

Historians would later call this the Prayer Meeting Revival, one of the most significant seasons of spiritual awakening in American history — begun not by a famous preacher, but by one ordinary layman's willingness to pray. Lanphier led the daily meeting for thirty-six years, retiring only in 1893. He died in 1898, having spent his life demonstrating that revival is not manufactured by human skill, but given by God — and simply asked for, in faith, on one's knees.

---

## Jim Elliot (1927–1956)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=jim-elliot
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jim_Elliot
Nationality: American · Tradition: Plymouth Brethren · Region: Latin America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: martyrdom, missions, gospel, faithfulness, sacrifice

Philip James Elliot was born in Portland, Oregon in 1927. He committed himself to missionary work before he left for Ecuador. Before going he wrote in his journal: "He is no fool who gives what he cannot keep to gain what he cannot lose."

He and four fellow missionaries — Nate Saint, Roger Youderian, Peter Fleming, and Ed McCully — attempted contact with the Waodani people, an isolated tribe that had killed all outsiders. After months of cautious approach, all five landed on a sandbar on the Curaray River in January 1956. Four days of apparently peaceful contact ended on 8 January when all five were killed. Jim Elliot was twenty-eight.

Elisabeth Elliot and Rachel Saint eventually went to live among the Waodani. The tribe that killed five missionaries became a Christian community — including some of the very men who had carried out the killings.

---

## John Bunyan (1628–1688)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=john-bunyan
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Bunyan
Nationality: English · Tradition: Nonconformist / Baptist · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 17th Century
Topics: perseverance, suffering, faithfulness, grace, gospel

John Bunyan was born in 1628 in Elstow, Bedfordshire, the son of a tinker. He came to faith through years of anguished spiritual struggle — tormented by the fear that he had sold his soul beyond recovery. The eventual resolution — the settled conviction that Christ's blood was more than sufficient — transformed both his inner life and his preaching.

Following the Restoration of Charles II, he was arrested for preaching without a licence and spent twelve years in Bedford Gaol. During a second imprisonment in 1676–77 he wrote The Pilgrim's Progress — an allegory of the Christian life following a man named Christian from the City of Destruction to the Celestial City. It is the second most translated book in history after the Bible.

The theology is unambiguous: Christian's burden falls from his shoulders not through human effort but at the foot of the cross — through faith in the crucified and risen Christ.

---

## John Calvin (1509–1564)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=john-calvin
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Calvin
Nationality: French · Tradition: Reformed/Presbyterian · Region: Europe · Era: Reformation
Topics: Reformed theology, Scripture, sovereignty of God, grace, predestination, gospel
Note: Calvin was directly involved in the trial and execution of Michael Servetus, who was burned at the stake in Geneva in 1553 for denying the Trinity. Calvin supported the execution and has been widely criticised for it. Capital punishment for heresy was widely accepted in that era and endorsed by other Reformers, but this does not diminish the gravity of the act. Calvin’s own writings on the episode reflect the moral blind spots of his time rather than his theology of grace.

John Calvin was born in Noyon, Picardy, France in 1509, and trained in law before what he called a “sudden conversion to teachableness” — a phrase that captures both his intellectual character and his sense that God had broken in unexpectedly. Forced to flee France after Protestant sympathies were linked to a controversial university address, he was persuaded to settle in Geneva in 1536.

His Institutes of the Christian Religion, first published that same year and expanded through several editions, became the most influential work of Protestant systematic theology. At its heart was the sovereign grace of God — that salvation is entirely God’s work, given freely through faith in Christ’s atoning death, received by none who deserve it, and secured by God’s own faithfulness to his own purposes.

In Geneva, Calvin preached through entire books of the Bible systematically, trained ministers for the French Reformed churches, corresponded with Reformers across Europe, and in 1559 founded the Geneva Academy — a seminary that shaped Reformed theology for generations.

He died in May 1564, aged fifty-four, worn out by relentless labour. His final instructions were that he be buried in an unmarked grave — a deliberate choice that God alone must be glorified.

His Institutes remain a theological landmark. His influence on Reformed and Presbyterian Christianity worldwide cannot be overstated.

---

## John Charles Ryle (1816–1900)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=john-charles-ryle
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J._C._Ryle
Nationality: English · Tradition: Anglican evangelical · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 19th Century
Topics: holiness, providence, suffering, conversion

John Charles Ryle was born in 1816 into a wealthy banking family and seemed destined for cricket, politics, or business — until two events overturned his plans. At Oxford in the winter of 1837, hearing Ephesians 2:8 read aloud in church, he was struck by the truth that salvation is “by grace... through faith... not of works, lest any man should boast.” Then, in 1841, his father's bank collapsed, wiping out the family fortune and closing off every worldly path he had imagined for himself.

Ryle took holy orders instead, later calling the bank's failure “the greatest earthly blessing” of his life, because it forced him toward the ministry God had actually intended. He served parishes in Suffolk for nearly forty years, writing plainly and forcefully on personal holiness and the authority of Scripture — his Expository Thoughts on the Gospels and Holiness are still read today. In 1880 he became the first Bishop of Liverpool, a new industrial diocese, where he worked to plant churches among the urban poor.

Ryle never treated his ruin as a tragedy to resent. He treated it as evidence that God's plans for a Christian's usefulness often run directly through the collapse of a person's own plans for themselves — and that grace, not a family fortune or a career, was what had truly carried him. He died in 1900.

---

## John G. Paton (1824–1907)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=john-g-paton
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_G._Paton
Nationality: Scottish · Tradition: Reformed Presbyterian Church of Scotland · Region: Oceania · Era: 19th Century
Topics: missions, perseverance, suffering, faithfulness, gospel

John Gibson Paton was born in 1824 to a devout farming family in Dumfriesshire, Scotland, where his father kept a ‘prayer closet’ and led the household in worship morning and night. Trained in Glasgow, he was ordained in 1858 and sailed with his young wife, Mary, for the island of Tanna in the New Hebrides (now Vanuatu) — known then for cannibalism and unceasing tribal warfare. Within a year Mary and their infant son were dead of illness, and Paton buried them himself, alone, in a land he had barely learned to call home.

He stayed. For years he lived under constant threat of death — clubbed, shot at, and once held at gunpoint for hours by a chief who eventually lowered his musket. Forced to flee Tanna in 1862 after an epidemic turned islanders against the missionaries, Paton remarried and, from 1866, gave nearly thirty years to the neighbouring island of Aniwa, reducing its language to writing and completing a translation of the New Testament in 1899. He tirelessly campaigned across Australia, Britain, and America, raising support for mission ships and speaking against the trafficking of Pacific islanders as forced labour.

Paton later wrote that in his darkest hours on Tanna, with a gun levelled at his chest, he was sustained not by his own courage but by the nearness of Christ, who had promised never to leave him. It was that promise, not his own endurance, that Paton credited for every soul reached.

---

## John Hus (c. 1369–1415)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=john-hus
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jan_Hus
Nationality: Bohemian · Tradition: Pre-Reformation reformer · Region: Europe · Era: Reformation
Topics: martyrdom, faithfulness, gospel, suffering, persecution

Jan Hus was born around 1369 in Husinec, Bohemia. He became rector of the University of Prague and preached at the Bethlehem Chapel in Czech rather than Latin, making his sermons accessible to ordinary people. Influenced by John Wycliffe, he preached against clerical corruption and for the authority of Scripture over the institutional hierarchy of Rome.

He was excommunicated in 1411. The Council of Constance summoned him; he came under an imperial safe-conduct promise, which was not honoured. He refused to recant teachings he held to be grounded in Scripture and was burned at the stake on 6 July 1415.

Hus is best understood as a pre-Reformation reformer who saw that the church must be reformed by Scripture alone. He died for that conviction a century before the Reformation gave it its fullest expression.

---

## John Knox (c. 1514–1572)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=john-knox
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Knox
Nationality: Scottish · Tradition: Presbyterian · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: Reformation
Topics: Reformation, preaching, courage, perseverance, gospel
Note: Knox published "The First Blast of the Trumpet Against the Monstrous Regiment of Women" (1558), arguing that rule by female monarchs was contrary to nature and Scripture. Aimed at Catholic queens Mary I of England and Mary of Guise, the tract nonetheless caused Knox lasting trouble — including with the Protestant Elizabeth I — and reflects a view of women in authority not shared by later Reformed tradition.

John Knox was born around 1514 near Haddington, Scotland, and trained as a Catholic priest before the preaching of the martyr George Wishart turned him toward reform. When Wishart was arrested, Knox stood ready to guard him with a sword — but was kept back from the execution that followed in 1546. The next year Knox himself was captured at St Andrews Castle and spent nineteen months chained as a French galley slave, suffering for a faith he had barely begun to preach.

After his release, Knox ministered in England and studied under John Calvin in Geneva before returning to Scotland in 1559 to lead its Reformation. His preaching helped topple the old order; he co-drafted the Scots Confession of 1560, which abolished papal authority and the Mass, and he is remembered as the founder of Presbyterianism in Scotland.

Through repeated confrontations with the crown, including several tense audiences with Mary, Queen of Scots, Knox held to one unmoving conviction: that a sinner is justified before God through faith in Christ's finished work alone — not through priest, sacrament, or royal favour. He preached at St Giles' in Edinburgh almost to the day he died in 1572. Fittingly for a man who sought no monument to himself, his grave now lies beneath a car park behind the church he served — his legacy resting not in stone, but in the Reformed faith he helped establish across a nation.

---

## John Nelson Darby (1800–1882)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=john-nelson-darby
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Nelson_Darby
Nationality: Irish · Tradition: Plymouth Brethren · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 19th Century
Topics: Scripture, prophecy, church, discipleship
Note: Darby's doctrine that all Christendom was in a state of irreparable 'ruin' — requiring total separation from denominations — has been rejected by most evangelical traditions. His ecclesiology caused significant divisions, including the permanent rupture between the Open and Exclusive Brethren in 1848. These views should be assessed carefully.

John Nelson Darby was born in London in 1800 and raised in Ireland. Trained as a lawyer, he was ordained in the Church of Ireland, then resigned his orders in 1828 — convinced that the institutional church had departed from New Testament patterns.

He became a key figure in the early Plymouth Brethren, gathering believers around Scripture and the Lord's Table without clergy or fixed liturgy. He travelled ceaselessly — Britain, France, Germany, North America, New Zealand — preaching and planting assemblies. His translations of the Bible into English, French, and German remain in use today.

Darby developed what became known as dispensationalist theology — a structured reading of biblical history that shaped much of later evangelical eschatology, particularly in North America. He believed Scripture was the church's only authority and that Christ's return was imminent.

His conviction that all denominations were irreparably corrupted led to painful divisions, including a rupture with George Müller and the Open Brethren in 1848. His legacy is contested, but his devotion to Scripture and his missionary energy were genuine. He died in 1882 having translated the Bible into three languages and planted churches across the world.

---

## John Newton (1725–1807)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=john-newton
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Newton
Nationality: English · Tradition: Anglican evangelical · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 18th Century
Topics: grace, conversion, redemption, missions
Hymns: Amazing Grace, Glorious Things of Thee Are Spoken, How Sweet the Name of Jesus Sounds, Amazing Grace (My Chains Are Gone), Great God of Wonders

John Newton was born in London in 1725. His devout mother died when he was six. The faith she had planted faded through a turbulent youth at sea, until by his mid-twenties he was working in the slave trade, openly contemptuous of religion.

The turning point came in March 1748, during a violent storm off Newfoundland. Convinced the ship would sink, Newton cried out to God. He later wrote that he could not claim full conversion in that moment — but it was the beginning of a long awakening. Ordained in 1764, he served at Olney, co-producing the Olney Hymns with William Cowper in 1779. In later years he gave evidence to Parliament during Wilberforce's abolitionist campaign.

Amazing Grace is the whole gospel in verse: not a good man who made mistakes, but a wretch reclaimed by sovereign mercy.

He died in 1807, having said: "I am a great sinner, and Christ is a great Saviour."

---

## John Stam (1907–1934)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=john-stam
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_and_Betty_Stam
Nationality: American · Tradition: Evangelical · Region: Asia · Era: 20th Century
Topics: martyrdom, missions, sacrifice, courage, faithfulness

John Stam was born in Paterson, New Jersey, in 1907, one of eleven children of a Dutch immigrant family. He came to faith through the Student Foreign Missions Fellowship and sailed for China in 1932 with the China Inland Mission. In 1933 he married Betty Scott, a fellow CIM missionary.

In December 1934 Communist soldiers captured John, Betty, and their two-month-old daughter Helen Priscilla in Jingde, Anhui province. John wrote a letter to CIM headquarters asking them not to spend effort or money on his ransom, trusting that God was in control. The following day he and Betty were marched through the streets and beheaded.

Helen was found alive two days later in their abandoned house, wrapped in blankets, left in the care of a local Christian. She survived.

John's life was brief but his testimony reached millions. His and Betty's letters and the account of their death were published immediately and circulated across the world, inspiring a generation of young missionaries. "Make me willing to be offered," he had prayed before sailing for China.

He was 27.

---

## John Sung 宋尚節 (1901–1944)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=john-sung
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Sung
Nationality: Chinese · Tradition: Methodist / independent evangelical · Region: Asia · Era: 20th Century
Topics: conversion, gospel, missions, faithfulness

John Sung was born in 1901 in Hinghwa, Fujian province, China. He earned a PhD in chemistry from Ohio State University in 1926. The years in America saw him drift from faith, engaging seriously with liberal theology.

His reconversion came in 1927 at a revival meeting in New York. Overwhelmed by a sense of sin and of Christ's forgiveness, he later burned his doctoral diploma as an enacted renunciation of worldly achievement — a testimony to 1 Corinthians 1:21.

He returned to China and conducted revival campaigns across China, Singapore, Indonesia, Malaya, the Philippines, and Taiwan, preaching with passionate urgency and calling people to genuine repentance and personal faith in Christ. Estimates suggest over 100,000 came to faith. He died in 1944, aged forty-two, exhausted by years of intensive ministry.

---

## John Wesley (1703–1791)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=john-wesley
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Wesley
Nationality: English · Tradition: Methodist/Anglican evangelical · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 18th Century
Topics: grace, conversion, revival, preaching, perseverance
Note: Wesley taught the doctrine of 'entire sanctification' or 'Christian perfection' — the belief that a second work of grace can enable believers to love God and neighbour as their governing motive. This is distinct from sinless perfection and does not contradict salvation by grace through faith, but it is a significant Arminian distinctive that Reformed and Calvinist evangelicals would question. His Aldersgate conversion and the thrust of his preaching were firmly evangelical.

John Wesley was born in Epworth, Lincolnshire in 1703, the fifteenth child of Samuel and Susanna Wesley. He studied at Oxford, was ordained an Anglican priest, and sailed to Georgia in 1735 as a missionary — a venture that ended in failure and his return to England deeply uncertain about his own standing before God.

The turning point came on 24 May 1738. At a Moravian meeting in Aldersgate Street, London, Wesley heard Luther's preface to Romans read aloud. He wrote afterwards: “I felt my heart strangely warmed. I felt I did trust in Christ, Christ alone for salvation.” From that evening he preached the grace he had previously only theorised. With the open fields as his pulpit — following George Whitefield’s example — he preached more than 40,000 sermons over fifty years, travelling 250,000 miles on horseback throughout Britain and Ireland. His Methodist societies became the seed of a global movement.

A week before his death in 1791 he wrote his final letter to William Wilberforce, urging him not to abandon the fight against slavery. He died at eighty-seven, still trusting the Christ whose grace had warmed his heart fifty-three years before. For all his formidable organisation and energy, the thing that drove him was a single conviction: that a grace sufficient for John Wesley was sufficient for anyone.

---

## John Wycliffe (c. 1328–1384)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=john-wycliffe
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Wycliffe
Nationality: English · Tradition: Pre-Reformation reformer · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: Reformation
Topics: gospel, faithfulness, persecution, Scripture, reformation

John Wycliffe was born around 1328 in Yorkshire and became one of Oxford’s foremost scholars — a philosopher, theologian, and doctor of divinity. He became rector of Lutterworth in Leicestershire in 1374, a post he held until his death.

He is called the “Morning Star of the Reformation” for the convictions he held a century before Luther: that Scripture holds supreme authority over church and pope, that salvation rests on God’s grace and election rather than on the church’s sacramental system, and that every person deserves to hear God’s Word in their own language. He challenged the doctrine of transubstantiation and denounced clerical corruption openly. In 1377 he was summoned before the Archbishop of Canterbury at St Paul’s Cathedral in London — the proceedings collapsed in disorder, and he returned to Lutterworth unmolested.

His most enduring legacy was to begin translating the Bible into Middle English. His writings directly shaped John Hus, and through Hus the whole Protestant Reformation. William Tyndale, who completed the first printed English New Testament, followed a path Wycliffe had opened.

The Council of Constance condemned him posthumously in 1415. In 1428 his bones were exhumed and burned, his ashes cast into the River Swift. As the river carried his ashes to the sea, so his doctrine spread across the world.

---

## Jonathan Edwards (1703–1758)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=jonathan-edwards
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jonathan_Edwards_(theologian)
Nationality: American · Tradition: Congregationalist · Region: North America · Era: 18th Century
Topics: grace, sovereignty of God, revival, conversion, preaching, beauty of God
Note: Edwards owned at least six enslaved people during his lifetime, including a woman named Venus purchased in 1731, and in 1741 wrote a pamphlet defending slavery in the American colonies while opposing the Atlantic slave trade. This was a grave moral failure inconsistent with the gospel of grace he preached, and is acknowledged here plainly alongside his theological legacy.

Jonathan Edwards was born in East Windsor, Connecticut, in 1703, the only son among eleven children of a Congregationalist minister. He entered Yale College at twelve. For years the Reformed doctrine of predestination struck him as harsh — until 1721, when reading the words "unto the King eternal, immortal, invisible, the only wise God, be honour and glory for ever" brought what he called "a new sense" of God's glory unlike anything he had known before. In 1727 he married Sarah Pierpont and was ordained pastor at Northampton, Massachusetts, succeeding his grandfather.

Edwards became one of colonial America's most significant theologians. His preaching sparked revival in Northampton in the 1730s, an early spark of the First Great Awakening across New England. His 1741 sermon "Sinners in the Hands of an Angry God" remains among the most famous in American history, warning hearers not toward despair but toward mercy freely offered in Christ. His writings defended one conviction: salvation is God's gift from beginning to end, received by faith alone, not secured by human effort.

In 1747 Edwards took the dying missionary David Brainerd into his home, sitting with him through his final weeks. After Brainerd's death he edited and published his diary, which went on to inspire generations of missionaries. Dismissed from his pulpit in 1750 over a dispute about church membership, Edwards spent his last years as missionary to the Housatonic people at Stockbridge, dying in 1758 days after being installed as president of the College of New Jersey.

---

## Joni Eareckson Tada (b. 1949)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=joni-eareckson-tada
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joni_Eareckson_Tada
Nationality: American · Tradition: Reformed evangelical · Region: North America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: suffering, providence, disability, grace, perseverance

Joni Eareckson Tada was born in 1949 in Baltimore, Maryland. In July 1967, at the age of 17, she dived into the Chesapeake Bay and struck the bottom — breaking her neck and leaving her a quadriplegic.

The years that followed were among the darkest of her life. She wrestled with depression, rage, and a desire to die. She was sustained by friends who refused to leave, by Scripture, and by a slow, painful re-engagement with a God whose purposes she could not yet see. She emerged not with easy answers, but with a conviction — deepened across six decades — that suffering is not evidence of God's absence but the terrain on which he is most clearly known.

She founded Joni and Friends in 1979, a ministry now active worldwide serving people with disabilities and their families. She has authored over fifty books, recorded music, and painted with a brush held in her mouth. She has also endured breast cancer and chronic pain.

She has said publicly that heaven is more real to her than her wheelchair — not as an escape from suffering, but as the resolution of a story she believes God is still writing.

---

## Joseph M. Scriven (1819–1886)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=joseph-scriven
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joseph_M._Scriven
Nationality: Irish-Canadian · Tradition: Plymouth Brethren · Region: North America · Era: 19th Century
Topics: suffering, prayer, comfort, humility, service, hymns, friendship with God
Hymns: What a Friend We Have in Jesus

Joseph Medlicott Scriven was born in 1819 in Banbridge, County Down, Ireland, the son of a Royal Marines officer. Educated at Trinity College Dublin, he seemed destined for a comfortable life. Instead, grief would mark his path. The night before his wedding in 1843, his fiancée drowned. Shattered, Scriven emigrated to Canada the following year, drawn by his Plymouth Brethren faith and a resolve to live wholly for Christ.

He settled in rural Ontario, teaching school, preaching, and devoting himself quietly to serving the poor — chopping wood for widows, helping those who could not pay, giving away what little he owned. He never sought prominence. He lived among the people he served.

In 1855, hearing that his mother in Ireland was gravely ill, Scriven wrote her a poem of comfort — privately, with no thought of publication. That poem became “What a Friend We Have in Jesus,” one of the most beloved hymns in the world. When a neighbour found the handwritten manuscript during his final illness and asked who had written it, Scriven replied: “The Lord and I did it between us.”

A second fiancée died of pneumonia before they could marry. He continued to serve, teach, and give until his own death by drowning in 1886, aged sixty-six. His gravestone carries the words: “Blessed are the pure in heart for they shall see God.”

Scriven found in Christ the friend the world could not be, and spent his life pointing others to the same.

---

## Joseph Parker (1830–1902)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=joseph-parker
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joseph_Parker_(theologian)
Nationality: English · Tradition: Congregationalist · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 19th Century
Topics: preaching, perseverance, reconciliation

Joseph Parker was born in 1830 in Hexham, Northumberland, the son of a stonemason, with no advantages of wealth or connection. He entered Congregationalist ministry in his early twenties, serving churches in Banbury and Manchester before returning to London in 1869 to lead the historic Poultry Chapel congregation.

Parker's preaching — vivid, extemporaneous, delivered without notes — drew such crowds that his congregation built the City Temple on Holborn Viaduct, opened in 1874, one of the great nonconformist pulpits of Victorian London. He was a contemporary and, at times, public rival of Charles Haddon Spurgeon — the two sparred in print over points of doctrine and style. Yet Spurgeon later praised Parker's originality generously, and Parker was a guest of honor at Spurgeon's fiftieth birthday celebration, the two having set aside their earlier friction. Historians remember Parker as “a pillar of orthodoxy” — a description that mattered more after his death, when his own successor at City Temple embraced the theological liberalism (“New Theology”) that Parker himself had never taught.

Parker's 25-volume The People's Bible preached through the whole of Scripture verse by verse. Whatever his combative streak in argument, the substance he defended in his years at the City Temple never moved: that the Bible's authority stood, and that sinners are reconciled to God only through Christ. He died in 1902.

---

## Katharina von Bora (c. 1499–1552)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=katharina-von-bora
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Katharina_von_Bora
Nationality: German · Tradition: Lutheran · Region: Europe · Era: Reformation
Topics: grace, marriage and family, reformation, faithfulness

Katharina von Bora was born around 1499 in Saxony and, like many daughters of the minor German nobility, was placed in a convent as a child — eventually a Cistercian house at Nimbschen. Reading the reforming writings circulating out of Wittenberg convinced her and eleven other nuns that convent vows could not earn the righteousness Martin Luther preached as a free gift in Christ.

On Holy Saturday, 1523, the twelve women escaped, smuggled out hidden among herring barrels in a merchant's wagon arranged by Luther. Two years later, in 1525, Katharina married Luther himself. She ran their household in Wittenberg's former monastery — farming, brewing, and keeping boarders — freeing Luther to write and teach, and in doing so helped establish the pattern of the Protestant pastor's family still recognizable today.

Widowed in 1546, she lost much of Luther's estate to legal disputes and fled Wittenberg twice during war and plague, finally dying in 1552 from injuries sustained escaping the second time. She is remembered to have said on her deathbed, “I will stick to Christ as a burr to cloth” — the same confidence in Christ's finished work, not her own record of labor or suffering, that had carried her out of the convent thirty years before.

---

## Keith Green (1953–1982)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=keith-green
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Keith_Green
Nationality: American · Tradition: Evangelical (Jesus Movement) · Region: North America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: gospel, grace, worship, repentance, discipleship, surrender
Hymns: Asleep in the Light, Create in Me a Clean Heart, Grace by Which I Stand, I Make My Life a Prayer to You, Oh Lord, You're Beautiful, So You Wanna Go Back to Egypt, There Is a Redeemer, Your Love Broke Through

Keith Gordon Green (1953–1982) was born in Brooklyn, New York, to a Jewish family. He showed extraordinary musical gifts from childhood — signed to ASCAP at eleven, contracted to Decca Records before his teens. But he spent his adolescence searching: drugs, Eastern mysticism, spiritual restlessness. In May 1975 he and his wife Melody attended a home Bible study in Beverly Hills and heard the gospel — that Jesus was the Jewish Messiah who had died for their sins. They believed it. Green was twenty-one.

What followed was one of the most concentrated outpourings of Christian music in the modern church. He released six studio albums, including songs that still appear in contemporary worship: There Is a Redeemer, Oh Lord You're Beautiful, Grace by Which I Stand, and Asleep in the Light. In 1977 he and Melody founded Last Days Ministries in Lindale, Texas, a communal outreach housing recovering addicts and others in need. In 1979 he decided he could not charge for his albums when the gospel itself was free — and mortgaged his home to finance distributing them without charge.

On 28 July 1982, he died in a plane crash near Garden Valley, Texas. Two of his young children, Josiah and Bethany, died with him. He was twenty-eight. In seven years of Christian faith he had called the sleeping church to wake up — and pointed it always to the Redeemer.

---

## Leon Morris (1914–2006)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=leon-morris
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leon_Morris
Nationality: Australian · Tradition: Evangelical Anglican · Region: Oceania · Era: 20th Century
Topics: Scripture, gospel, grace, biblical scholarship, cross

Leon Morris was born in 1914 in Lithgow, New South Wales, and trained as a teacher before entering ministry, ordained a deacon in the Anglican Diocese of Sydney in 1938. Topping the national theology exam and serving first as a curate and then as a ‘bush pastor’ in outback South Australia, he went on to earn a PhD at Cambridge with a dissertation that would become one of the most significant evangelical books of the twentieth century.

That book, The Apostolic Preaching of the Cross (1955), argued — against a rising tide of theological fashion — that the New Testament presents Christ’s death as a real substitution, in which the Son bore the wrath that sinners deserved. Morris’s summary of the doctrine became famous: ‘Either we die, or He dies.’ He served as Warden of Tyndale House, Cambridge, and then for fifteen years as Principal of Ridley College, Melbourne, training generations of Australian clergy, while writing more than fifty books, including a widely used commentary on John’s Gospel, and helping translate the NIV and ESV Bibles.

For Morris, the cross was never a puzzle to be admired from a safe distance. It was the place where God himself, in Christ, paid what sinners could never pay — so that grace, not human effort, is the only ground on which anyone stands before God.

---

## Lilias Trotter (1853–1928)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=lilias-trotter
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lilias_Trotter
Nationality: British · Tradition: Interdenominational evangelical · Region: Africa · Era: 19th Century
Topics: missions, sacrifice, perseverance, prayer, faith, surrender

Lilias Trotter (1853–1928) was a British artist who, in her twenties, was told by art critic John Ruskin — the most celebrated critic of the Victorian age — that she had the potential to become the greatest living painter. It was not flattery: he offered to train her personally.

She declined. Converted through D.L. Moody's London meetings in the 1870s, she had concluded that no calling, however glittering, could compete with the eternal. She wrote: "I cannot give myself to painting in the way he means and continue to seek first the Kingdom of God." She served among London's poor, then in 1888 sailed to Algeria — rejected by the North Africa Mission on health grounds, she went anyway with two friends and founded what became the Algiers Mission Band.

She spent forty years among Muslim Algerians, learning Arabic, forming relationships across vast cultural distance, bearing witness over a long obedience. She never saw mass conversion. She kept painting — now as worship rather than ambition — and her devotional writings, including Parables of the Cross, remain in print. She died in Algeria in 1928. Her life poses the question she posed herself: what is more valuable — the fame that perishes, or the fruit that abides? She answered it with forty years of faithful service in a place that returned almost nothing visible.

---

## Lord Shaftesbury (1801–1885)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=lord-shaftesbury
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_7th_Earl_of_Shaftesbury
Nationality: English · Tradition: Anglican evangelical · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 19th Century
Topics: justice, prayer, obedience, stewardship, faith, compassion

Anthony Ashley Cooper, 7th Earl of Shaftesbury (1801–1885), was one of England's most aristocratic figures who entered Parliament in 1826 and devoted sixty years there to the protection of the vulnerable. His evangelical faith, formed in youth, was the explicit foundation of everything he did. "I can do nothing without prayer," he wrote in his diary. "I begin every day of my public life with prayer."

He is best known for the Factory Acts of 1833, 1844, and 1850, which limited working hours for children in mills; the Mines Act of 1842, which prohibited children under ten and all women from working underground; and the Chimney Sweepers Act of 1875, which ended the practice of sending small boys up chimneys. He was President of the Ragged Schools Union from 1844, overseeing free education for thousands of the poorest children in Britain, and championed legislation transforming the treatment of the mentally ill.

As a child, Shaftesbury was largely unloved by his parents. His warmest memory was his family's housekeeper, Maria Millis, who read Scripture with him. When she died she left him her watch, which he wore every day for the rest of his life. When he died in 1885, the streets were lined not by lords but by the working poor, who had come to say goodbye to the man they considered their champion.

---

## Louis Zamperini (1917–2014)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=louis-zamperini
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Louis_Zamperini
Nationality: American · Tradition: Presbyterian / evangelical · Region: North America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: grace, conversion, forgiveness, suffering, perseverance

Louis Zamperini was born in 1917 in Olean, New York, and grew up to become a champion distance runner, competing in the 5,000 metres at the 1936 Berlin Olympics. During the Second World War he served as a bombardier in the US Army Air Forces. In May 1943 his B-24 crashed into the Pacific; he survived forty-seven days adrift on a life raft before being captured by Japanese forces. For nearly two years he was held as a prisoner of war, enduring brutal treatment, particularly under a guard named Mutsuhiro Watanabe.

Zamperini returned home from the war outwardly a hero but inwardly consumed by nightmares, rage, and alcoholism. In October 1949 he attended Billy Graham's Los Angeles crusade. Graham's preaching on the cross moved him to surrender his life to Christ, and that night his nightmares stopped for good. In 1952 he founded Victory Boys Camp to mentor troubled youth, and spent the rest of his life speaking about the faith and forgiveness that had remade him.

In 1998, at eighty years old, Zamperini returned to Japan hoping to personally forgive his former captors, including Watanabe, who refused to meet him. He forgave him anyway. Zamperini was clear that this forgiveness was not something he had willed into being through sheer determination — it was the same grace he had received at the foot of the cross, now flowing outward to men who had shown him none.

---

## Martin Luther (1483–1546)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=martin-luther
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Martin_Luther
Nationality: German · Tradition: Lutheran · Region: Europe · Era: Reformation
Topics: Reformation, grace, Scripture, faith, suffering, gospel
Hymns: A Mighty Fortress Is Our God
Note: Luther’s later writings, especially On the Jews and Their Lies (1543), contain virulent antisemitic rhetoric that has no biblical warrant and has caused immense historical harm. These writings stand in stark contradiction to his theology of grace and represent a serious moral failure. They are condemned by Lutheran churches worldwide. His central theological contributions — sola fide, sola gratia, sola scriptura — remain foundational to Protestant Christianity.

Martin Luther was born in 1483 in Eisleben, Saxony, the son of a copper miner who sent him to study law. During a violent thunderstorm in 1505, fearing sudden death, Luther vowed to become a monk. He entered an Augustinian monastery and pursued holiness with terrifying intensity — yet found only despair. However exhaustive his penance, he could not silence the voice that told him God was against him.

The breakthrough came through Scripture. Studying Paul’s letter to the Romans, he wrestled with the phrase the righteousness of God — and finally understood it not as the righteousness that condemns, but as the righteousness given freely to the sinner through faith in Christ. “I felt myself to be reborn and to have gone through open doors into paradise,” he wrote.

On 31 October 1517 he posted ninety-five theses against the sale of indulgences — the spark that became the Protestant Reformation. Summoned before the Diet of Worms in 1521, he refused to recant unless shown by Scripture and plain reason that he was wrong: “Here I stand; I can do no other.”

He translated the New Testament into German in eleven weeks (1522), giving ordinary people direct access to Scripture. He married Katharina von Bora in 1525 and modelled a new pattern of Christian domestic life.

He died in Eisleben in February 1546 — in the town where he was born. He left behind a Reformation built on a single rediscovery: by faith alone.

---

## Martyn Lloyd-Jones (1899–1981)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=martyn-lloyd-jones
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Martyn_Lloyd-Jones
Nationality: Welsh · Tradition: Calvinistic Methodist / independent evangelical · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 20th Century
Topics: Scripture, preaching, gospel, grace

David Martyn Lloyd-Jones was born in Cardiff, Wales, in 1899 and trained as a doctor, rising quickly to become chief clinical assistant to the physician who attended the royal family. But treating patients who faced death with every medical resource available and no lasting hope unsettled him. In his mid-twenties, moved by preaching he heard and by the emptiness he saw in dying rooms that medicine could not touch, he came to faith in Christ and, in 1927, gave up a highly promising medical career to become a minister.

He served first at a small Calvinistic Methodist chapel in Aberavon, Wales, and from 1938 at Westminster Chapel, London, becoming one of the century's most influential preachers. Lloyd-Jones preached for decades through entire books of the Bible — Romans, Ephesians, the Sermon on the Mount — in long, doctrinally rich sermons a colleague called ‘logic on fire,’ insisting that people needed not entertainment but the gospel itself: that sinners are justified before God by faith in Christ alone, not by their own effort. He helped found the Banner of Truth Trust and the London Theological Seminary to keep that same gospel being taught after him.

In 1966 he controversially urged evangelicals to leave denominations that no longer held that gospel clearly — a call publicly opposed by his friend John Stott. Whatever one makes of that disagreement, both men agreed on what mattered most: that the good news of grace, not the shape of any denomination, is what saves.

---

## Mary Slessor (1848–1915)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=mary-slessor
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mary_Slessor
Nationality: Scottish · Tradition: United Presbyterian · Region: Africa · Era: 19th Century
Topics: missions, faithfulness, justice, perseverance, sacrifice

Mary Slessor was born in Aberdeen in 1848. Her father was an alcoholic shoemaker; the family moved to Dundee where Mary worked in a jute mill from age eleven, twelve-hour shifts, six days a week. Converted through the United Presbyterian Church and inspired by the death of David Livingstone, she offered herself for missionary service in West Africa.

She arrived in Old Calabar, Nigeria in 1876. She mastered the Efik language and moved progressively deeper inland than any previous missionary, eventually living among the Okoyong people far from any European presence. She wore local dress, went barefoot, and lived as her neighbours lived.

In the Calabar region, twins were believed to be cursed. One or both infants would be killed; the mother driven out. When Slessor heard the screaming she ran — sometimes barefoot through the undergrowth — to rescue the babies. She adopted many and raised them herself.

In 1892 she was appointed magistrate of the Court of Equity in Okoyong — the first woman to hold such a role in the British Empire. She died in Nigeria in 1915, having served for thirty-nine years.

A factory girl from Dundee, without formal education or social influence, who changed the culture of a region by refusing to look away from its cruelties.

---

## Matthew Henry (1662–1714)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=matthew-henry
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Matthew_Henry
Nationality: British · Tradition: Presbyterian/Nonconformist · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 17th Century
Topics: Scripture, exposition, prayer, devotion, grace

Matthew Henry was born in 1662 at Broad Oak Farm, Flintshire, Wales, the son of Philip Henry — a Nonconformist minister ejected from his parish that very year under the Act of Uniformity. From childhood he was formed by Scripture and by the cost of holding convictions under pressure.

Ordained in 1687, he ministered at Chester for twenty-five years, then moved to Hackney, London in 1712. He was known as a pastor who preached the whole counsel of God with warmth and loved his people faithfully.

His great work was his Exposition of the Old and New Testaments — a commentary on the entire Bible begun around 1704 and left unfinished at his death in 1714 when he fell from his horse near Nantwich. Colleagues completed the final volumes from his notes. It became the most widely read English Bible commentary ever written — not because of academic learning, but because of its devotional warmth and its consistent habit of pointing every passage to Christ.

His approach has endured for three centuries: Scripture speaks to the heart, not merely the intellect; every part of the Bible speaks of Christ; and the aim of Bible study is not to master the text but to be mastered by it. He wrote as a pastor who loved the souls before him.

The discovery that drove his life was simple: the grace of God in Christ is the beginning and end of all Scripture.

---

## Menno Simons (1496–1561)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=menno-simons
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Menno_Simons
Nationality: Dutch · Tradition: Anabaptist (Mennonite) · Region: Europe · Era: Reformation
Topics: Reformation, grace, nonresistance, discipleship
Note: Menno Simons founded a lasting Anabaptist tradition built on believer's baptism, rigorous church discipline, and mandatory nonresistance (a rejection of violence, including self-defense and military service) as conditions of church membership. These are distinctive positions not shared across evangelical traditions, including church discipline practiced as the collective “ban” — withdrawal of fellowship from the unrepentant by the whole congregation, including family members. This does not place Menno Simons outside historic Christian orthodoxy, but readers should know these positions are debated among evangelicals.

Menno Simons was born in 1496 in Witmarsum, Friesland, and ordained a Roman Catholic priest around 1515. Doubts about transubstantiation set him studying Scripture on his own, and in 1531 the execution of a man for practicing adult baptism forced him to examine what the Bible said about the rite.
