His crisis deepened in 1535, when his brother Pieter was killed in a violent Anabaptist uprising at Oldeklooster — a tragedy that convinced Menno the movement needed leaders committed to peace, not the sword.

On 12 January 1536, Menno renounced the priesthood and, soon after, was baptized as an adult believer. He spent the next twenty-five years as a hunted preacher across the Low Countries and northern Germany, organizing scattered Anabaptist congregations into a stable, disciplined movement — one so shaped by his leadership its members came to be called “Mennonites.” He taught that true faith “manifests itself in all righteousness and works of love... feeds the hungry; consoles the afflicted; shelters the miserable,” insisting a living faith in Christ must show itself in a transformed life.

Menno died in 1561, having spent his ministry arguing the church should follow Christ's example of nonresistance rather than the violence some radical Anabaptists had embraced. His convictions on baptism and discipline remain distinctive within the wider Christian family — see the footnote below — but his central confession matched the one held across the Reformation: sinners are saved by grace through faith in Christ, not any rite the church performs.

---

## Mitsuo Fuchida (1902–1976)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=mitsuo-fuchida
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mitsuo_Fuchida
Nationality: Japanese · Tradition: Nondenominational evangelical · Region: Asia · Era: 20th Century
Topics: grace, conversion, forgiveness, redemption, gospel
Note: Fuchida commanded the first wave of the December 7, 1941 attack on Pearl Harbor, in which over 2,400 Americans were killed, before his 1949 conversion to Christianity. This is noted here plainly, without minimizing the loss of life or treating his later profession of faith as erasing his role in it.

Mitsuo Fuchida was born in 1902 in Nara Prefecture, Japan, and rose through the Imperial Japanese Navy Air Service to become one of its most skilled pilots. On 7 December 1941 he commanded the first wave of aircraft against Pearl Harbor, radioing back the signal confirming surprise had been achieved. He later flew at the Battle of Midway, where he was severely wounded, and served out the war as a staff officer.

Demobilized after Japan's surrender, Fuchida carried the weight of a war that had cost so many lives. In the late 1940s he learned of Peggy Covell, an American whose missionary parents had been killed by Japanese soldiers, yet who chose to care for Japanese prisoners of war out of love for Christ. Soon after, he read the testimony of Jacob DeShazer, an American airman brutally treated as a Japanese prisoner, who had come to faith in Christ in captivity and forgiven his captors. Fuchida began reading the Bible himself — and in September 1949, he became a Christian. He spent his life as an evangelist, touring under the title 'From Pearl Harbor to Calvary,' and became a close friend of Jacob DeShazer, at times preaching alongside people his nation had once fought.

Fuchida never claimed his past could be undone by anything he did. What changed him was not remorse turned into good works, but grace — the same grace he had first seen lived out by people he had every reason to expect would hate him.

---

## Nate Saint (1923–1956)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=nate-saint
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nate_Saint
Nationality: American · Tradition: Interdenominational evangelical · Region: Latin America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: missions, sacrifice, martyrdom, faithfulness, obedience

Nate Saint was born in 1923 near Hershey, Pennsylvania. Prevented by a childhood bone condition from completing wartime flight training, he instead put his piloting skill to use for the gospel, joining Mission Aviation Fellowship and, in 1948, opening its jungle base at Shell Mera, Ecuador, with his wife Marjorie.

Saint pioneered the 'spiraling line' technique — circling a small plane tightly enough that a bucket on a long line would hang almost motionless over one spot below — allowing him to lower gifts to isolated tribes who had never seen an outsider survive contact with them. Through these patient, prayerful gift-drops, Saint and four fellow missionaries — Ed McCully, Roger Youderian, Peter Fleming, and Jim Elliot — began building trust with the Huaorani people of the Ecuadorian rainforest, a group who had killed every outsider who had approached them before.

On 8 January 1956, having camped for days on a Curaray River sandbar in what appeared to be peaceful contact, Saint and his four companions were speared to death by Huaorani warriors. He was thirty-two.

Saint's death did not end the story. His sister Rachel Saint, and later his own son Steve, went to live among the very people who had killed him — and the gospel he had risked everything to carry took root. Christ's call to lay down one's life for others, he had believed, was not a metaphor.

---

## Nicholas Ridley (c. 1500–1555)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=nicholas-ridley
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nicholas_Ridley_(martyr)
Nationality: English · Tradition: Anglican Reformer · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: Reformation
Topics: martyrdom, Scripture, grace, courage

Nicholas Ridley was born around 1500 in Northumberland and educated at Pembroke College, Cambridge, before further study at the Sorbonne in Paris. He returned to England a scholar of unusual depth, was ordained, and became chaplain to Archbishop Thomas Cranmer in 1537 — a connection that placed him at the center of England's Reformation. He served as Master of Pembroke College, then as Bishop of Rochester, and from 1550 as Bishop of London, where his preaching and writing helped establish Reformed doctrine in the English church, particularly on the nature of the Lord's Supper.

Ridley's support for the ill-fated attempt to place Lady Jane Grey on the throne made him politically vulnerable the moment Mary I secured the crown. Tried for heresy alongside Cranmer and Hugh Latimer, he was burned at the stake on 16 October 1555, outside Balliol College, Oxford, together with Latimer.

It was Ridley's own comrade Latimer, seeing him waver, who offered the words of comfort that have echoed ever since: “Be of good comfort, and play the man, Master Ridley; we shall this day light such a candle, by God's grace, in England, as I trust shall never be put out.” Ridley found the courage those words called for, dying beside his friend for a conviction he had spent his career defending — that sinners stand before God clothed in Christ's righteousness, not their own.

---

## Nicky Cruz (b. 1938)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=nicky-cruz
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nicky_Cruz
Nationality: Puerto Rican · Tradition: Assemblies of God / Pentecostal · Region: Latin America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: grace, conversion, forgiveness, redemption, evangelism
Note: Cruz has ministered within the Assemblies of God and the broader Pentecostal tradition, which holds that believers may receive a subsequent 'baptism in the Holy Spirit,' evidenced by speaking in tongues, distinct from conversion. This is a doctrinal position not shared across all evangelical traditions and is noted here for readers to weigh alongside his testimony.

Nicky Cruz was born on 6 December 1938 in Las Piedras, Puerto Rico, into a family involved in spiritism; his mother once told him he was destined to serve Satan. At fifteen he was sent to live with a brother in New York City, but he ran away and fell in with street gangs, rising within months to become warlord and later president of the Mau Maus, one of Brooklyn's most feared gangs.

In 1958, Cruz went to a rally held by a young Pentecostal preacher named David Wilkerson, who had come to the city specifically to reach gang members like him. Cruz had mocked and threatened Wilkerson before, but that night he broke down and gave his life to Christ. He trained at Bible college, served alongside Wilkerson in Teen Challenge — a ministry to addicts and gang members — and later founded his own evangelistic ministry, Nicky Cruz Outreach. He told his story in the bestselling autobiography Run Baby Run (1968), later adapted into the film The Cross and the Switchblade.

Cruz has spent more than six decades preaching in dozens of countries, always returning to the same point: that the change in his life was not something he worked up in himself. A boy raised to believe he belonged to darkness was met, instead, by a grace he had done nothing to deserve — and it was that grace, not his own resolve, that set him free.

---

## Oswald Chambers (1874–1917)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=oswald-chambers
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oswald_Chambers
Nationality: Scottish · Tradition: Baptist / Holiness movement, nondenominational · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 20th Century
Topics: devotion, faithfulness, Scripture, grace, service

Oswald Chambers was born in Aberdeen, Scotland, in 1874, and baptized at sixteen into a London Baptist chapel. Trained first as an artist and then in theology at Edinburgh and Dunoon, he became a gifted, searching Bible teacher, eventually founding and leading the Bible Training College in Clapham, London, from 1911 to 1915. In 1910 he married Gertrude ‘Biddy’ Hobbs, and their daughter Kathleen was born three years later.

In 1915, with the college's future uncertain amid the First World War, Chambers became a YMCA chaplain to British, Australian, and New Zealand troops stationed near Cairo, Egypt — walking among soldiers awaiting battle, teaching them the same searching faith he had taught his students. In October 1917 he underwent an emergency appendectomy; complications followed, and he died on 15 November 1917, only forty-three years old, with no book to his name.

That might have been the end of his story. Instead, his widow Biddy — who had taken careful shorthand notes of his oral talks for years — spent the following decade shaping them into a book, published in 1924 as My Utmost for His Highest. It has never been out of print since, read daily by millions in dozens of languages. Chambers himself never saw a page of it. His enduring legacy is, quite literally, not his own doing — a fitting shape for a man who taught, above all, that a Christian's usefulness rests entirely on Christ's grace, never on human achievement or length of days.

---

## Pandita Ramabai (1858–1922)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=pandita-ramabai
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pandita_Ramabai
Nationality: Indian · Tradition: Independent evangelical · Region: Asia · Era: 19th Century
Topics: missions, grace, Scripture, social reform, women

Pandita Ramabai was born in 1858 into a high-caste Brahmin family in India. Her father, a Sanskrit scholar, unusually taught her to read the sacred texts — a radical act in a culture that barred women from learning. Both parents died in famine, and she became celebrated as a Sanskrit scholar in Calcutta.

After years of searching in Hinduism for relief from its oppression of women, she encountered the Gospels. She was baptised as a Christian in 1883 in England, having come to believe that Christ alone offered dignity to the low-caste, the widow, and the outcast — a conviction tested and deepened throughout her life.

She founded the Mukti ("Salvation") Mission near Pune as a refuge for child widows, famine victims, and women in poverty. In 1905 Mukti became a centre of revival — prayer and conversion spreading through hundreds of women and girls.

She also produced a Marathi translation of the Bible from the original Hebrew and Greek, completing it shortly before her death in 1922.

Her life proclaimed what the gospel insists: that in Christ there is no Brahmin and no outcaste — only the redeemed.

---

## Peter Fleming (1928–1956)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=peter-fleming
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pete_Fleming
Nationality: American · Tradition: Plymouth Brethren · Region: Latin America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: missions, sacrifice, martyrdom, faithfulness, gospel

Peter ('Pete') Fleming was born in 1928 in Seattle, Washington. A gifted student of philosophy at the University of Washington, where he led the university's Christian fellowship, Fleming might have pursued an academic career — but in 1952 he sailed for Ecuador instead, alongside his close friend Jim Elliot, as a missionary with the Plymouth Brethren agency Christian Missions in Many Lands.

Fleming served among the Quichua people at Shandia, learning their language in the field, and married Olive Ainslie in 1954 after Elliot encouraged him to give his first year of ministry undivided attention. Of the five men who later attempted contact with the Huaorani — Elliot, Nate Saint, Ed McCully, and Roger Youderian among them — Fleming was the only one without children of his own.

On 8 January 1956, as the group was attacked on a Curaray River sandbar, Fleming was the second to die, killed while attempting to call out friendly words in the Huaorani language.

Fleming had exchanged the promise of an academic future for a harder and more hidden calling. He had come to believe, and to live out, the truth Scripture teaches plainly: that a life is not made valuable by what it achieves, but is given its worth entirely by the Christ who redeems it through faith, not through merit.

---

## Philip Bliss (1838–1876)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=philip-bliss
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philip_Bliss
Nationality: American · Tradition: Baptist / evangelical · Region: North America · Era: 19th Century
Topics: gospel, grace, perseverance, suffering
Hymns: Almost Persuaded, Wonderful Words of Life, Man of Sorrows What a Name, Let the Lower Lights Be Burning, Hallelujah, What a Savior!, I Will Sing of My Redeemer, Jesus Loves Even Me

Philip Paul Bliss was born in 1838 in Clearfield County, Pennsylvania, in rural poverty. He became a music teacher, came to faith in Christ, and later served as song leader and composer for D.L. Moody's evangelistic campaigns.

His gospel songs — Almost Persuaded, Wonderful Words of Life, Hallelujah What a Saviour — were clear, accessible, and Christ-centred. He also set to music words not his own: Horatio Spafford, who had lost all four daughters in a shipwreck, wrote, "When sorrows like sea billows roll; whatever my lot, thou hast taught me to say, it is well with my soul." Bliss's tune made them unforgettable.

Bliss and his wife died on 29 December 1876 in the Ashtabula River rail disaster, aged thirty-eight. God uses ordinary instruments for extraordinary purposes.

---

## Philip Doddridge (1702–1751)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=philip-doddridge
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philip_Doddridge
Nationality: English · Tradition: Congregationalist · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 18th Century
Topics: education, grace, hymn writing, revival
Hymns: O Happy Day, Hark the Glad Sound!, Awake, My Soul, Stretch Every Nerve, O God of Bethel

Philip Doddridge was born in London in 1702, the last of twenty children, and orphaned by thirteen. He was taken in by a Presbyterian minister to whom he later wrote, “To him under God I owe even myself and all my opportunities of public usefulness in the church.” He trained for ministry rather than accepting an offer to fund his studies at Cambridge, and in 1729 became pastor of an independent congregation in Northampton, where he also ran a dissenting academy that trained ministers excluded from England's universities by their nonconformity.

Doddridge wrote over four hundred hymns, among them “O Happy Day,” “Hark the Glad Sound!,” and “Awake, My Soul, Stretch Every Nerve” — hymns of vigorous, active faith that nonetheless rest entirely on grace already given rather than earned. His prose work The Rise and Progress of Religion in the Soul (1745) walked readers through conversion and Christian growth so effectively that Charles Spurgeon later called it “that holy book,” and it was translated into seven languages.

Doddridge died of tuberculosis in Lisbon in 1751, where he had traveled seeking a warmer climate for his health, and was buried in the city's British Cemetery. He left behind students trained for gospel ministry and hymns still sung by congregations who have long since forgotten his name — proof that the “opportunities of public usefulness” he received from grace, he spent giving grace away.

---

## R. A. Torrey (1856–1928)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=ra-torrey
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reuben_Archer_Torrey
Nationality: American · Tradition: Congregationalist / nondenominational evangelical · Region: North America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: evangelism, prayer, fundamentals of the faith, missions, preaching

Reuben Archer Torrey was born in 1856 in Hoboken, New Jersey, and educated at Yale and Yale Divinity School before further theological study in Germany. He began as a Congregational pastor, but in 1889 was drawn into the orbit of evangelist Dwight L. Moody, becoming superintendent of the Bible institute Moody had founded in Chicago — training laypeople for ministry without requiring the university education few of them could afford.

From 1902 to 1907, Torrey led evangelistic campaigns across the English-speaking world and Asia, often alongside song leader Charles Alexander, drawing crowds that rivaled Moody's own. But his more lasting legacy was institutional rather than personal: he became founding dean of the Bible Institute of Los Angeles (BIOLA) in 1912, and from 1910 served as one of the editors of The Fundamentals — an essay series, distributed free to hundreds of thousands of pastors and workers, that defended the deity of Christ, the authority of Scripture, and salvation through Christ's atoning death against the rising theological liberalism of the day. The word “fundamentalist” itself traces back to this project.

Torrey was as insistent on prayer as on doctrine, writing extensively to urge believers to depend on the Holy Spirit rather than mere technique or personality in evangelism — a caution aimed, not least, at platform evangelists like himself. He died in 1928, having spent a working life making sure that whatever methods changed, the message underneath — Christ crucified and risen for sinners — would not.

---

## R.C. Sproul (1939–2017)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=rc-sproul
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/R._C._Sproul
Nationality: American · Tradition: Reformed/Presbyterian · Region: North America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: grace, Scripture, sovereignty of God, holiness, teaching

R.C. Sproul was born in 1939 in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. As a student at Westminster College, he was challenged by another student's plain, direct witness about Christ, and in 1957 committed his life to him. Sproul later recalled the moment as the beginning of a lifelong pursuit of knowing God more truly.

After theological study, including under G.C. Berkouwer in the Netherlands, Sproul devoted himself to teaching ordinary Christians the doctrines that had gripped him: the holiness and sovereignty of God, the authority of Scripture, and justification by faith alone. In 1971 he founded a small study centre in rural Pennsylvania that grew into Ligonier Ministries, reaching a global audience through books, radio, and recorded teaching. His best-known book, The Holiness of God (1985), retold his own realization that a right view of who God is exposes exactly how little any person could ever deserve God's favour.

In 1978 Sproul helped draft the Chicago Statement on Biblical Inerrancy, defending the trustworthiness of Scripture as the foundation for knowing Christ rightly at all. Until his death in 2017, he insisted that sinners are not saved by their own goodness, however sincere, but by grace alone, through faith alone, in Christ alone — the same conviction that had taken hold of him as a young student decades before.

---

## Richard Wurmbrand (1909–2001)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=richard-wurmbrand
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Richard_Wurmbrand
Nationality: Romanian · Tradition: Lutheran evangelical · Region: Europe · Era: 20th Century
Topics: persecution, suffering, faithfulness, perseverance, martyrdom

Richard Wurmbrand was born in Bucharest, Romania in 1909 to a Jewish family. In his early adult years he was a Communist and an atheist. His conversion came in the 1930s through the witness of a Romanian peasant carpenter and an Anglican missionary.

During the Nazi occupation he worked clandestinely to shelter Jewish people and minister to German soldiers. When the Communists took power, he continued underground church work. On 29 February 1948 he was arrested and held for fourteen years — three in complete solitary confinement underground, subjected to systematic torture designed to break the human person entirely.

Released in 1964 and ransomed to the West, he founded Voice of the Martyrs in 1967 and wrote Tortured for Christ. He died in 2001 at ninety-one, saying that in the worst of what he had endured, Christ was present.

---

## Robert Boyle (1627–1691)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=robert-boyle
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robert_Boyle
Nationality: Irish · Tradition: Anglican Protestant · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 17th Century
Topics: creation, faith, Scripture, prayer, stewardship

Robert Boyle (1627–1691) was an Anglo-Irish natural philosopher and one of the most important scientists of the seventeenth century. Born at Lismore Castle, Ireland, he settled in Oxford in the early 1650s and conducted systematic experiments into the properties of gases, formulating what became known as Boyle's Law. He was a founder of the Royal Society and is widely regarded as a father of modern chemistry — the first to define elements, compounds, and mixtures in terms still recognisable today.

What distinguished Boyle from many contemporaries was the depth of his Christian conviction. He was not a deist. He believed in the God of Scripture — personal, providential, revealed in Christ — and spent years learning Hebrew and Greek to read the Bible in its original languages. He financed translations of the New Testament into Irish, Turkish, and Malay, and the Old Testament into Welsh, so that more people might read it.

In The Christian Virtuoso he argued that scientific inquiry was an act of worship: the natural world was a second book written by God, and to study it was to trace the mind of the Creator. He endowed the Boyle Lectures — an annual series defending Christianity against unbelief — which continued for three centuries. Boyle demonstrated by his life the argument he made in his writing: the more carefully he examined creation, the more clearly he saw the Creator.

---

## Robert Cleaver Chapman (1803–1902)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=robert-cleaver-chapman
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robert_Cleaver_Chapman
Nationality: British · Tradition: Open Brethren · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 19th Century
Topics: love, holiness, prayer, generosity, missions

Robert Cleaver Chapman was born in 1803 in Elsinore, Denmark, to an English merchant family. Converted in his early twenties, he trained as a solicitor, gave up a promising legal career, and in 1832 settled in Barnstaple, Devon, where he would minister for the next seventy years.

He became associated with the Open Brethren and was known across the evangelical world for a rare combination of doctrinal solidity and extraordinary love. George Müller, who knew him well, called him "the saintliest man I ever knew." Men travelled to Barnstaple simply to observe how he lived. His home was open to all — he fed the hungry, housed the homeless, and corresponded with missionaries across the globe.

Chapman lived to 99 and never retired from ministry. He was a poet, an evangelist, and a letter-writer whose counsel was sought by leaders across denominations. His maxim was that love is the greatest apologetic: "Let your life preach, then let your lips, if necessary, also speak."

His life pointed to the grace that produces unhurried, unboastful, enduring love — not a love manufactured by duty, but one rooted in a sight of Christ he had been cultivating for eight decades.

---

## Robert Lowry (1826–1899)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=robert-lowry
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robert_Lowry_(hymn_writer)
Nationality: American · Tradition: Baptist · Region: North America · Era: 19th Century
Topics: worship, praise, grace, hymns, gospel
Hymns: Shall We Gather at the River, Nothing But the Blood of Jesus, Christ Arose! (Low in the Grave He Lay), Marching to Zion, I Need Thee Every Hour

Robert Lowry was born in 1826 in Philadelphia. Ordained into Baptist ministry in 1854, he served a series of churches over the following decades, including Hanson Place Baptist Church in Brooklyn and, later, Park Avenue Baptist Church in Plainfield, New Jersey, alongside a season teaching rhetoric at his alma mater.

Though not formally trained in music, Lowry composed several of the era's most enduring gospel hymn tunes. In 1864, reflecting on the river of life described in the book of Revelation, he wrote 'Shall We Gather at the River' — a hymn he later dismissed, with characteristic modesty, as 'brass band music,' even as congregations everywhere kept singing it. In 1876 he wrote both words and music for 'Nothing But the Blood of Jesus,' a hymn built entirely around a single, unadorned gospel claim: that sinners are cleansed not by their own effort but solely by what Christ accomplished on the cross.

Lowry also encouraged fellow hymn writers, including Fanny Crosby, for whom he composed tunes, and Annie Hawks, whose words he set to music for 'I Need Thee Every Hour.' He died in 1899, having spent a working life reminding ordinary congregations, in the simplest possible language, that nothing recommends a sinner to God except the blood of Christ, received by faith.

---

## Robert Murray M'Cheyne (1813–1843)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=robert-murray-mcheyne
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robert_Murray_M%27Cheyne
Nationality: Scottish · Tradition: Presbyterian · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 19th Century
Topics: holiness, prayer, revival, missions, sanctification
Hymns: When This Passing World Is Done

Robert Murray M'Cheyne was born in Edinburgh in 1813 and converted in his late teens following the death of his older brother. He studied at Edinburgh University under Thomas Chalmers, was licensed to preach in 1835, and ordained as minister of St. Peter's, Dundee, in 1836 at the age of 23.

In the seven years of his ministry he became one of the most remarkable preachers Scotland had known. His power in the pulpit was widely attributed to his extraordinary prayer life and rigorous personal holiness — he is said to have risen at five each morning to pray before studying and preaching. In 1839 he joined a delegation to Palestine to survey the prospects for Jewish mission, and on his return found that revival had broken out among his congregation in his absence.

He died in 1843, aged 29, from typhus contracted while visiting sick parishioners during an epidemic. His friend Andrew Bonar's Memoir and Remains, published shortly after, became one of the most influential books in Scottish evangelical history.

M'Cheyne's life is a rebuke and a comfort — a rebuke to the idea that long years are needed for God's purposes, and a comfort that fruitfulness flows from proximity to Christ. "Lord, make me as holy as a pardoned sinner can be" was his prayer.

---

## Roger Youderian (1924–1956)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=roger-youderian
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roger_Youderian
Nationality: American · Tradition: Baptist evangelical · Region: Latin America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: missions, sacrifice, martyrdom, faithfulness, conversion

Roger Youderian was born in 1924 in Sumatra, Montana. He served as a US Army paratrooper in the Second World War, fighting at the Battle of the Bulge and in Operation Varsity — and it was in England, through the influence of an army chaplain, that he professed faith in Christ in the autumn of 1944.

After the war, Youderian trained at Northwestern College in Minnesota and married Barbara Orton. In 1953 the couple arrived in Ecuador, where Youderian began missionary work among the Jivaro (Shuar) people at the remote Macuma station — a people known, like the neighbouring Huaorani, for a history of violence toward outsiders.

In January 1956, Youderian joined Nate Saint, Ed McCully, Peter Fleming, and Jim Elliot in a careful attempt at peaceful contact with the Huaorani. On 8 January, as the group was attacked on a Curaray River sandbar, Youderian was the last of the five to die, killed while trying to radio for help.

A former soldier who had once fought for his country, Youderian had come to believe that the greater battle was a spiritual one, already won at the cross — and that no one, soldier or missionary, is saved by their own courage, but only by faith in the One who fought and died for them.

---

## Rosetta Sherwood Hall (1865–1951)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=rosetta-sherwood-hall
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rosetta_Sherwood_Hall
Nationality: American · Tradition: Methodist Episcopal · Region: Asia · Era: 20th Century
Topics: compassion, perseverance, sacrifice, service

Rosetta Sherwood Hall was born in 1865 in Liberty, New York, and trained as a doctor at the Woman's Medical College of Pennsylvania. In 1890 she sailed to Korea as a medical missionary, and there married fellow physician William James Hall. Widowed at twenty-nine when William died of typhus caught while tending wounded soldiers, Rosetta — six weeks pregnant — briefly returned to America, then chose to go back to Korea with her small children rather than remain safely home.

Her infant daughter Edith died of dysentery soon after their return. Rather than retreat, Rosetta founded a children's medical ward in Edith's name, then went on to establish the Pyongyang School for the Deaf and Blind in 1909, pioneering Korean Braille, and in 1928 co-founded Korea's first women's medical school. She served Korea for forty-four years. Her son Sherwood Hall later became a missionary doctor himself, launching Korea's first Christmas Seals campaign against tuberculosis.

Rosetta could have called her husband's death, and her daughter's, reasons to stop. Instead she turned grief into decades of gospel-shaped service, trusting that the God who had not spared her losses was still worth giving her whole life to — not because service earns His favor, but because grace received is grace given away.

---

## Sadhu Sundar Singh (1889–c. 1929)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=sadhu-sundar-singh
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sadhu_Sundar_Singh
Nationality: Indian · Tradition: Independent evangelical · Region: Asia · Era: 20th Century
Topics: conversion, missions, gospel, suffering
Note: Singh's mystical experiences and some unverifiable accounts are viewed with caution by some evangelical believers. Not all reported events in his life can be corroborated, and some should be received with appropriate critical discernment. His gospel preaching was clearly Christ-centred.

Sundar Singh was born in 1889 in Rampur, in the Himalayan foothills. His mother died when he was approximately fourteen, precipitating a spiritual crisis. He became hostile to Christianity, burning pages from a New Testament.

On 18 December 1904, he arose before dawn intending to throw himself under a train. He prayed. A figure appeared — radiant, speaking in Hindustani: "How long will you persecute me? I have come to save you." He identified the figure as Jesus, and his life was transformed.

He became a sadhu — wearing the saffron robe, living on hospitality — and preached the gospel across India, Tibet, and Southeast Asia.

Note: Some accounts associated with Singh cannot be independently verified and should be received with critical discernment.

He disappeared in 1929 while attempting to cross the Himalayas into Tibet, aged approximately forty.

---

## Samuel Ajayi Crowther (c. 1809–1891)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=samuel-ajayi-crowther
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Samuel_Ajayi_Crowther
Nationality: Nigerian (Yoruba) · Tradition: Anglican (Church Missionary Society) · Region: Africa · Era: 19th Century
Topics: freedom, missions, translation, perseverance

Samuel Ajayi Crowther was born around 1809 in Osogun, in what is now Nigeria. Around age twelve he was captured during a raid, separated from his mother, and sold into slavery. The Portuguese ship carrying him was intercepted by a Royal Navy anti-slavery patrol, and he was landed at Freetown, Sierra Leone, where Church Missionary Society schools gave him an education he had never expected to receive. He was baptized in 1825.

Crowther became the first student at Fourah Bay College, was ordained, and gave himself to translating Scripture into his native Yoruba, completing the full Bible in 1884 alongside a Yoruba grammar and prayer book. In 1864 he was consecrated the first African bishop of the Anglican Church, at Canterbury Cathedral, and led the CMS Niger Mission for decades — though in his final year, younger European missionaries undermined his authority in ways historians now recognize as racially motivated.

In 1846, returning as a missionary to the region of his childhood, Crowther was reunited with his mother, twenty-five years after the raid that had torn them apart. She and other family members subsequently believed in Christ. The boy stolen from his home became, by grace alone, the means by which his own family was found by the God he had come to know in exile.

---

## Samuel Lamb 林献羔 (1924–2013)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=samuel-lamb
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Samuel_Lamb
Nationality: Chinese · Tradition: Independent evangelical (Baptist background) · Region: Asia · Era: 20th Century
Topics: perseverance, persecution, faithfulness, suffering

Samuel Lamb — known in Chinese as Lin Xiangao — was born in 1924 near Guangzhou, the son of a Baptist pastor. He trained for ministry in Hong Kong and began preaching in Guangzhou in the years before Communist rule, in the same circles as the independent evangelist Wang Ming Dao.

When China's new government demanded that every Protestant church submit to the state-controlled Three-Self Patriotic Movement, Lamb refused, insisting that Christ alone governs His church. He was imprisoned in 1955, then re-arrested in 1958 and sentenced to twenty years of hard labor, much of it spent coupling coal wagons in freezing underground mine tunnels where many men around him were killed or maimed. Released in 1979, he reopened his small house church in Guangzhou; within a few years it was drawing thousands of worshippers across several services each week, entirely without official recognition.

Looking back on the mines, Lamb said only that God had protected him though he could have died many times over. He liked to sum up his life in one line: more persecution, more growth. What carried him through two decades of imprisonment was not his own endurance but the same gift he preached every week — that God receives sinners not for their compliance or their merit, but freely, by grace, through faith in Christ alone.

---

## Samuel Medley (1738–1799)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=samuel-medley
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Samuel_Medley_(minister)
Nationality: English · Tradition: Particular Baptist · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 18th Century
Topics: conversion, grace, perseverance, gospel
Hymns: I Know That My Redeemer Lives, Awake, My Soul, in Joyful Lays

Samuel Medley was born in 1738 in Cheshunt, Hertfordshire. Apprenticed as a teenager to an oilman, he escaped the trade by enlisting in the Royal Navy, and was severely wounded at the Battle of Lagos in 1759. Expecting to lose his injured leg, he read a sermon by Isaac Watts while recovering and was converted — the surgery, in the end, proved unnecessary, but the change in his life was permanent.

After running a school in Soho for several years, Medley joined a Baptist congregation in London, began preaching in 1766, and was ordained two years later. In 1772 he became pastor of Byrom Street Baptist Church in Liverpool, a bustling port city, where he ministered for twenty-seven years — with particular care for the sailors passing through, men who reminded him of the wounded young man he had once been. He published roughly 230 hymns, including “I Know That My Redeemer Lives” and “Awake, My Soul, in Joyful Lays,” hymns critics judged more earnest than polished but which congregations sang for generations regardless.

Medley's own testimony ran through his ministry like a refrain: a sailor who expected to lose a limb instead found a Redeemer. He spent the rest of his life telling other wounded, wandering people that the same grace that met him on a hospital bed was available to them too.

---

## Samuel Rutherford (c. 1600–1661)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=samuel-rutherford
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Samuel_Rutherford
Nationality: Scottish · Tradition: Covenanter / Presbyterian · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 17th Century
Topics: suffering, prayer, exile, devotion, Christ's love
Note: Rutherford wrote A Free Disputation Against Pretended Liberty of Conscience (1649), arguing that civil authorities should suppress false religion and explicitly opposing the religious toleration advocated by Roger Williams. This defense of state-enforced conformity is rejected by the great majority of evangelical Christians today, who hold liberty of conscience to be a biblical and moral good. His devotional writing and pastoral suffering remain widely valued.

Samuel Rutherford was born around 1600 in the Scottish Borders and rose to become one of the most learned ministers in the Church of Scotland, serving the parish of Anwoth in Galloway from 1627. His preaching and pastoral visiting were tireless, but his refusal to conform to the bishops' ceremonies brought him under repeated suspicion from the church courts.

In 1636 he was formally barred from ministry and banished to Aberdeen “during the King's pleasure” — an open-ended exile for nonconformity. It was there, cut off from his congregation, that he wrote most of the devotional Letters for which he is remembered: intimate, Christ-absorbed reflections on suffering that Charles Spurgeon later called “the nearest thing to inspiration...in all the writings of mere men.” Restored to ministry after 1638, he went on to represent Scotland at the Westminster Assembly and to write Lex, Rex (1644), a defense of constitutional government that was publicly burned after the Restoration.

Cited to face a charge of treason for that book, Rutherford died in St Andrews in 1661 before the case could proceed, reportedly answering the summons: “I have got a summons already before a superior Judge, and I behove to answer my first summons.” His life's whole direction, like his last recorded words, turned away from his own record toward Christ alone — “Acquainted with Emmanuel's love” is carved on his grave.

---

## Son Yang-won 손양원 (1902–1950)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=son-yang-won
Wikipedia: https://ko.wikipedia.org/wiki/손양원
Nationality: Korean · Tradition: Presbyterian · Region: Asia · Era: 20th Century
Topics: forgiveness, persecution, martyrdom, suffering

Son Yang-won was born in 1902 in Gyeongsangnam-do, Korea. Trained at Pyongyang Theological Seminary, he became pastor of the church at Aeyangwon, a leprosy colony near Yeosu, where he spent decades caring for patients that much of society had abandoned. In 1940, Japanese colonial authorities imprisoned him for about five years for refusing to bow at Shinto shrines.

In 1948, during the Yeosu-Suncheon rebellion, a young communist sympathizer murdered Son's two sons, Dong-in and Dong-shin, for being Christians loyal to the new government. When the killer was captured and sentenced to die, Son did the almost unthinkable: he pleaded for the young man's life, and then adopted him as his own son. Two years later, during the Korean War, Son was captured by retreating Communist troops, tortured, and shot near Yeosu — killed, like his sons, for his faith.

Son's adoption of his sons' killer was not sentiment; it was doctrine made visible. He had spent his ministry telling leprosy patients that God does not love the deserving — He loves and forgives sinners freely, through faith in Christ, who first forgave those who killed Him. Son simply lived out, at unbearable cost, the grace he had always preached.

---

## Susanna Wesley (1669–1742)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=susanna-wesley
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Susanna_Wesley
Nationality: English · Tradition: Anglican · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 18th Century
Topics: faithfulness, prayer, perseverance

Susanna Wesley was born in London in 1669, the twenty-fifth child of a Nonconformist minister. She married Samuel Wesley and the couple served the parish of Epworth in Lincolnshire, where nineteen children were born to them, ten surviving infancy.

In circumstances of poverty, Samuel's imprisonment for debt, and a fire that destroyed the rectory, Susanna devoted one hour per week to individual spiritual instruction with each child: Scripture, the catechism, and personal conversation about faith.

The fruit is visible. John Wesley became the founder of Methodism; Charles Wesley became the most prolific hymn writer in the English language. Both explicitly credited their mother as the primary influence on their faith and ministry.

Susanna published very little. But her invisible faithfulness shaped what may be the most significant renewal movement in 18th-century Christianity.

---

## Thomas Barnardo (1845–1905)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=thomas-barnardo
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thomas_Barnardo
Nationality: Irish/British · Tradition: Evangelical · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 19th Century
Topics: mercy, service, children, faith, compassion, missions

Thomas John Barnardo was born in Dublin in 1845 and converted to evangelical faith through the Plymouth Brethren at seventeen. Moved by news of the Taiping Rebellion, he came to London in 1866 intending to train as a doctor before sailing to China as a missionary. The children of the East End changed his plans.

On a winter evening, a boy called Jim Jarvis showed Barnardo a rooftop in Stepney where eleven homeless children slept on bare tiles in the cold. Barnardo could not look away. He began holding ragged school classes, then opened a mission room, and in 1870 opened his first home for destitute boys with the now-famous notice above the door: “No destitute child ever refused admission.”

He founded a village home for girls at Barkingside in 1876 and eventually ran more than ninety homes across Britain. By his death in September 1905, nearly 60,000 children had been cared for. The organisation bearing his name continues to this day.

He never reached China. But he understood his life’s work as no less a mission: the destitute children of Britain’s cities were as much a calling from God as any foreign field.

The gospel drove his philanthropy. Grace received expressed itself in mercy given — and the door was never closed.

---

## Thomas Cranmer (1489–1556)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=thomas-cranmer
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thomas_Cranmer
Nationality: English · Tradition: Anglican Reformer · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: Reformation
Topics: martyrdom, Scripture, grace, courage

Thomas Cranmer was born in 1489 in Nottinghamshire and became a fellow of Jesus College, Cambridge, where sustained study of Scripture moved him toward the reforming convictions then spreading from the Continent. His path to church leadership began unusually: he suggested that Henry VIII's marriage question be settled by university theologians rather than Rome, a suggestion that led directly to his appointment as Archbishop of Canterbury in 1533, in the middle of Henry's break with the papacy.

As archbishop, Cranmer shaped the doctrine and worship of the newly independent English church for over twenty years. He wrote the Book of Common Prayer, putting worship into English so ordinary believers could pray, and hear Scripture read, in their own tongue. He drafted the Forty-two Articles, later revised into the Thirty-nine Articles that still define Anglican doctrine — grace through faith in Christ's finished work, not human merit, at their center.

When the Catholic Mary I came to the throne, Cranmer was imprisoned and, under pressure, signed recantations of his Protestant beliefs. But at the stake in 1556, he withdrew them, declaring that his hand — which had signed the recantations — would burn first, since it had offended. He held that hand steady in the flames. His last words were: “Lord Jesus, receive my spirit. I see the heavens open and Jesus standing at the right hand of God.” A man who had once bent under pressure died holding, without wavering, to the grace he had proclaimed.

---

## Thomas Watson (c. 1620–1686)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=thomas-watson
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thomas_Watson_(Puritan)
Nationality: English · Tradition: Puritan (Reformed / Nonconformist) · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 17th Century
Topics: grace, repentance, sovereignty, sanctification, gospel, perseverance

Thomas Watson (c.1620–1686) was an English Puritan minister educated at Emmanuel College, Cambridge — then the nursery of Puritan theology in England. He became rector of St Stephen Walbrook in the City of London in 1646, where he ministered with remarkable effectiveness for sixteen years. In 1662, the Act of Uniformity required all clergy to subscribe to the Book of Common Prayer and accept episcopal ordination. Watson refused. Along with nearly two thousand other Puritan ministers — in what became known as the Great Ejection — he was turned out of his church on a single day, 24 August 1662.

He is known chiefly for three books. A Body of Divinity — sermons on the Westminster Shorter Catechism — remains one of the clearest introductions to Reformed theology in the English language, opening with the question "What is the chief end of man?" and demonstrating that doctrine is not abstract but immediately connected to worship. All Things for Good meditates on Romans 8:28 with the authority of a man who had lost his living; The Doctrine of Repentance carefully distinguishes genuine repentance from its counterfeits.

Watson died suddenly while in prayer at Barnston, Essex in 1686. He was Reformed in theology, Puritan in devotion, and pastoral in care for ordinary believers — always intent on helping them understand not just what they believed, but how to tell whether they had the real thing.

---

## Toyohiko Kagawa 賀川豊彦 (1888–1960)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=toyohiko-kagawa
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Toyohiko_Kagawa
Nationality: Japanese · Tradition: Presbyterian / evangelical social reformer · Region: Asia · Era: 20th Century
Topics: compassion, social reform, suffering, service
Note: Kagawa's ministry was marked by a strong emphasis on Christian practice and social reform, which at times sat loosely to doctrinal precision — he described 'Christianity in action' as the truth behind Christian doctrine rather than doctrine itself. He also made some ambiguous public statements during Japan's wartime nationalism that have troubled later historians. These are noted for the reader's discernment; they do not erase a lifetime of sacrificial gospel service among the poor.

Toyohiko Kagawa was born in Kobe, Japan, in 1888. Discipled as a teenager by Southern Presbyterian missionaries, he was baptized in 1904 — a decision that cost him his family, who disowned him. He went on to study at Kobe Theological Seminary and Princeton, but it was not academic theology that shaped his calling.

On Christmas Eve 1909, while still a seminary student, Kagawa moved into Kobe's Shinkawa slum to live among its poorest residents, sharing his own meagre stipend and enduring beatings and robbery from the gangsters who controlled the district. From that single room he built a life's work: organizing Japan's labor movement, founding consumer cooperatives, and from 1926 leading the nationwide 'Kingdom of God' evangelistic campaign that carried the gospel into towns and villages across Japan. He was nominated for the Nobel Peace Prize and the Nobel Prize in Literature.

Kagawa insisted that Christianity had to be lived, not merely believed — but the gospel he lived out was still the old one: that Christ receives sinners freely, and asks them in turn to love their neighbor as he had loved them, without payment or precondition.

---

## W. E. Vine (1873–1949)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=william-edwy-vine
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/W._E._Vine
Nationality: British · Tradition: Open Brethren · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 20th Century
Topics: Scripture, doctrine, grace, discipleship, biblical scholarship

William Edwy Vine was born in 1873 in Somerset and educated at University College, Bristol. He spent his entire adult life in Open Brethren assemblies — teaching, preaching, and writing in the conviction that Scripture, rightly understood, was the church's sufficient guide.

He is best known for Vine's Expository Dictionary of New Testament Words, published in four volumes between 1939 and 1941. The Dictionary traces every significant word in the Greek New Testament back to its root, explaining its range of meaning in careful, plain English. For the first time, preachers and teachers without formal Greek training could access the depth of the original text — the difference between agapē and philia, between justification and righteousness, between knowledge that merely informs and wisdom that transforms.

Vine wrote it in the conviction that the words of Scripture were God-breathed in every detail, and that careful attention to them was an act of worship as much as an act of study. He completed the work in his late sixties, seeing it through to publication near the end of his life.

His name is not widely celebrated. But Vine's Dictionary sits on the shelves of preachers, teachers, and careful Bible readers around the world — a quiet instrument, still doing what he intended: placing the wealth of the New Testament's words within reach of those who love them.

---

## Wang Ming Dao 王明道 (1900–1991)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=wang-ming-dao
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wang_Mingdao
Nationality: Chinese · Tradition: Independent evangelical · Region: Asia · Era: 20th Century
Topics: perseverance, suffering, faithfulness, persecution

Wang Ming Dao was born in Beijing in 1900 and founded an independent house church there in 1925. He led it for thirty years on a single conviction: the church belongs to Christ and Scripture alone. When China's Communist government demanded all Protestant churches join the state-controlled Three-Self Patriotic Movement, Wang refused — he argued it had admitted those who denied the gospel.

He was arrested in 1955. Under sustained pressure he signed a recantation — and could not live with it. He described himself as a Peter who had denied his Lord, withdrew the confession in 1958, and returned to prison rather than live a lie. He spent twenty-three years imprisoned in total.

He emerged in 1980 elderly and nearly blind, yet unbroken in faith. He continued to encourage the underground church until his death in 1991.

His story is not one of seamless heroism but of grace: a man who fell, was restored, and held to the end — not by his own strength, but by the mercy of a Lord who does not abandon those who stumble back to Him.

---

## Watchman Nee 倪柝聲 (1903–1972)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=watchman-nee
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Watchman_Nee
Nationality: Chinese · Tradition: Independent evangelical (Little Flock) · Region: Asia · Era: 20th Century
Topics: suffering, persecution, faithfulness, gospel
Note: Nee's later ecclesiology and some teachings on church authority are not universally accepted among evangelical believers. His insistence on a strict 'one church per city' principle and related authority structures have been viewed with concern by many in the broader evangelical tradition. His gospel teaching and core devotional writings are widely valued.

Henry Nee Shu-Tsu, known as Watchman Nee, was born in Shantou, Guangdong, China in 1903. He came to faith at seventeen and devoted himself to intensive study of Scripture across Reformed, Keswick, and Brethren traditions.

In the 1920s and 1930s he developed the Little Flock movement — house church assemblies seeking to recover the New Testament pattern of church life. His writings, particularly The Normal Christian Life and Sit Walk Stand, have been widely valued for their exposition of the believer's union with Christ.

Note: Nee's ecclesiology — particularly his insistence on one church per city — is viewed with concern by many evangelical believers.

In 1952 he was arrested by the Communist authorities and spent the last twenty years of his life in prison. A note found by his pillow read: "Christ is the Son of God who died for the redemption of sinners and resurrected after three days. This is the greatest truth in the universe. I die because of my belief in Christ."

---

## William Booth (1829–1912)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=william-booth
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Booth
Nationality: British · Tradition: Methodist/Salvation Army · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 19th Century
Topics: mercy, service, evangelism, mission, compassion, suffering

William Booth was born in 1829 in Sneinton, Nottingham, into poverty, and converted to Methodist faith in his early teens. Apprenticed to a pawnbroker, he could not escape the sight of what grinding poverty did to people. He became a Methodist minister, then broke with the denomination to become an independent evangelist.

In 1865 he began preaching in the East End of London — in tents, pubs, and the street — to people no respectable church would have sought. He called his mission the East London Christian Mission; in 1878 it was renamed the Salvation Army, with Booth as General and a military structure that gave formerly aimless men and women rank, discipline, and purpose.

His method was simple and radical: reach the person where they are, in their language, and meet their physical need alongside their spiritual one. His 1890 book In Darkest England and the Way Out proposed a systematic plan to address poverty through hostels, labour colonies, and skills training — not as a substitute for the gospel but as its immediate expression.

By his death in 1912, the Salvation Army was operating in fifty-eight countries. Booth never lost the conviction that drove him from the start: Christ died for every human being without exception, including those every other institution had written off.

He once told his son: “While women weep as they do now, I’ll fight. While men go to prison, in and out, in and out, I’ll fight. I’ll fight to the very end.”

---

## William Borden (1887–1913)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=william-borden
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Borden_(missionary)
Nationality: American · Tradition: Presbyterian evangelical · Region: North America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: sacrifice, missions, faithfulness, gospel, surrender

William Borden was born in Chicago in 1887, heir to the Borden dairy fortune. At sixteen, his parents gave him a round-the-world trip. Confronted with poverty across the Middle East and Asia, he wrote home that he intended to give his life to missionary service. In his Bible he wrote two words: No reserves.

At Yale he was a gifted scholar and athlete who turned down lucrative prospects and gave away large portions of his inheritance to missions and Christian work. After Princeton Seminary he prepared to serve among Chinese Muslims in Kansu province. In his Bible: No retreat.

In 1912 he sailed to Egypt to learn Arabic on his way to China. He contracted cerebrospinal meningitis in Cairo and died on 9 April 1913, aged twenty-five, never having reached the mission field he had prepared for.

In his Bible, found after his death: No regrets.

Three phrases written across a decade, each at a different moment of cost — the testimony of a life held loosely from the beginning, released further at each step, and given completely at the last. Borden died in a hospital bed in Cairo. The phrase written at the end is what remains.

---

## William Carey (1761–1834)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=william-carey
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Carey_(missionary)
Nationality: English · Tradition: Baptist · Region: Asia · Era: 18th Century
Topics: missions, translation, perseverance, prayer, obedience, faith

William Carey (1761–1834) was an English Baptist cobbler from Northamptonshire who educated himself in Greek, Hebrew, Latin, and French from books he could barely afford. When he came to evangelical faith, he hung a handmade map of the world above his workbench and prayed over regions that had never heard the gospel. In 1792 he preached the sermon remembered for its watchword — "Expect great things from God; attempt great things for God" — and helped found the Baptist Missionary Society.

He sailed for India in 1793, spending forty-one years there without returning home. Working from Serampore, he translated the entire Bible into Bengali and portions into Sanskrit, Marathi, and over two dozen other languages, founded Serampore College — the first degree-granting institution in Asia — and campaigned successfully against sati, the burning of widows.

When fire destroyed the translation workshop in 1812, consuming years of manuscript work, Carey wrote: "The loss is heavy — but as travelling a road the second time is done with more confidence, I hope the work will lose nothing of real value." He rebuilt and continued. He is called the father of modern missions — not for exceptional gifts, but because he forced the church to ask whether obedience to the Great Commission was optional. His answer, grounded in Scripture, cost him everything — and remade Protestant mission.

---

## William Cowper (1731–1800)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=william-cowper
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Cowper
Nationality: English · Tradition: Anglican evangelical · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 18th Century
Topics: grace, suffering, perseverance, prayer
Hymns: God Moves in a Mysterious Way, There Is a Fountain Filled with Blood, O for a Closer Walk with God

William Cowper was born in 1731 in Berkhamsted, Hertfordshire. His mother died when he was six. He suffered a severe mental breakdown in 1763 including three suicide attempts and a settled conviction that he was eternally damned. Admitted to an asylum in St Albans, he came to quiet faith through reading Romans 3:25 — finding assurance that the propitiation Christ made was sufficient even for him.

Working with John Newton at Olney, he contributed sixty-seven hymns to the Olney Hymns (1779), including God Moves in a Mysterious Way and There Is a Fountain Filled with Blood. He never fully escaped the shadow of mental illness.

God Moves in a Mysterious Way has a remarkable origin: a man on the edge of breakdown writing of God's unfathomable purposes — "the clouds ye so much dread are big with mercy." The truth he expressed, even when he could not feel it, has comforted believers in darkness for two centuries.

---

## William Herbert Brewster Sr. (1897–1987)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=william-herbert-brewster
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Herbert_Brewster,_Sr.
Nationality: American · Tradition: Baptist · Region: North America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: gospel music, hope, grace, civil rights
Hymns: Move On Up a Little Higher, Surely, God Is Able, Let Us Go Back to the Old Landmark

William Herbert Brewster Sr. was born in 1897 on a sharecropping farm near Somerville, Tennessee, the oldest of eight children. He worked his way through Roger Williams College in Nashville and in 1930 became pastor of East Trigg Avenue Baptist Church in Memphis, a position he held for fifty-seven years.

Brewster wrote more than two hundred gospel songs and staged musical dramas that combined biblical themes with the hard realities of Black life under segregation. His 1946 song “Move On Up a Little Higher,” recorded by Mahalia Jackson, became the first gospel record to sell a million copies; “Surely, God Is Able” followed with the Ward Singers in 1949. His biographer records that he “constantly preached on the theme that a better day was coming, one in which all men could walk as brothers” — a hope he grounded not in politics but in the promise of Christ, who alone lifts the lowly.

A young Elvis Presley sat in Brewster's congregation and absorbed his radio broadcasts, carrying something of that sound into American popular music. But Brewster's own aim was narrower and higher: to give suffering people songs sturdy enough to stand on. “Move On Up a Little Higher” was not a call to climb by human effort — it was an invitation to the eternal reward Christ secures for those who trust him, sung by people who had precious little reason to hope in anything else.

---

## William MacDonald (1917–2007)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=william-macdonald
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_MacDonald_(Christian_author)
Nationality: American · Tradition: Plymouth Brethren · Region: North America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: Scripture, discipleship, grace, teaching, biblical scholarship

William MacDonald was born on 7 January 1917 in Leominster, Massachusetts. After earning degrees from Tufts College and Harvard Business School, he worked as an investment analyst at the First National Bank of Boston — but commerce gave way to conviction. He served in the Navy during the Second World War, and afterwards gave himself entirely to Bible teaching through the Plymouth Brethren.

From 1946 he served on the faculty of Emmaus Bible School in Oak Park, Illinois, becoming its president in 1959. After stepping down in 1965, he spent years teaching in the United States, Europe, and Asia, then joined the Discipleship Intern Training Program in California, which he served until 1996.

MacDonald published more than eighty books, but his lasting monument is the *Believer's Bible Commentary* (1989) — a single-volume exposition of every book of Scripture, written in plain and accessible prose. Where figures such as William E. Vine had mapped the language of the New Testament, MacDonald mapped the whole landscape of the Bible for the ordinary believer. His *True Discipleship* pressed the saved believer beyond nominal religion into whole-hearted allegiance — not confusing the cost of discipleship with the free gift of salvation, but refusing to accept that a faith with no fruit in one's life was saving faith at all.

He died on 25 December 2007, Christmas Day, having spent sixty years making the Word of Christ dwell richly in whoever would read.

---

## William Tyndale (c. 1494–1536)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=william-tyndale
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Tyndale
Nationality: English · Tradition: Protestant Reformer · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: Reformation
Topics: martyrdom, faithfulness, gospel, suffering, persecution

William Tyndale was born around 1494. He became convinced that the reformation of the English church required one thing above all: that ordinary English people should be able to read the Scriptures in their own language.

He sought ecclesiastical sponsorship in London and was refused. He left England in 1524, never to return, translated the New Testament from the original Greek, and arranged for printed copies to be smuggled back into England. The language he produced — direct, rhythmic, of extraordinary literary quality — formed the foundation of the King James Version of 1611.

In 1535 he was betrayed, arrested, and imprisoned in Vilvoorde Castle near Brussels. He was strangled and burned on 6 October 1536. His last recorded words: "Lord, open the King of England's eyes." Three years later, Henry VIII authorised Tyndale's Bible in every parish church in England.

---

## William Wilberforce (1759–1833)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=william-wilberforce
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Wilberforce
Nationality: English · Tradition: Anglican evangelical · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 18th Century
Topics: faithfulness, perseverance, grace, gospel

William Wilberforce was born in Hull, Yorkshire in 1759. He entered Parliament at twenty-one and was regarded as one of the most gifted politicians of his generation. In 1785 he underwent evangelical conversion — shaped in part by correspondence with John Newton — and became convinced God had called him to two great objectives: the abolition of the slave trade, and the reformation of public morals.

The campaign took twenty years. He brought his first abolition bill in 1788 and was defeated. He brought it again, repeatedly. The Slave Trade Act passed in 1807. The abolition of slavery throughout the British Empire was passed in 1833; he received the news three days before his death.

His faith was not incidental to his political work — it was its source. His life is a witness that patient faithfulness through twenty years of defeat is itself an act of trust in God.
